name: Arsenica Sans
classification_notes: A geometric sans-serif that retains Old Style and Art Nouveau calligraphic influences from its parent serif family, giving it a warmer, more characterful construction than a neutral geometric.
summary: A geometric sans with Art Nouveau and Old Style calligraphic ancestry, extending the Arsenica superfamily into a 21-style display and text system.
description: Francesco Canovaro's extension of the Arsenica serif superfamily into sans-serif territory retains the calligraphic trace of the original rather than stripping it away, with Art Nouveau and Old Style influences surfacing most clearly in the italics. The family spans Thin to Extrabold across two subfamilies: Arsenica Sans for editorial and display use with full italic complement, and Arsenica Sans Text with reduced stylistic detail for body setting. Nine stylistic sets, oldstyle figures, contextual alternates and a variable font make it a flexible system for identity work as much as editorial typesetting.
foundry_blurb: Zetafonts describe Arsenica Sans as an extension of the Arsenica superfamily into an Art Nouveau-infused geometric sans, preserving the calligraphic spirit and eclectic sensibility of the original. The design carries Old Style and Art Nouveau influences into a sans-serif framework, with two subfamilies covering editorial, display and text use cases, a rich OpenType feature set, and coverage of over 216 languages across Latin and Cyrillic scripts.

name: Tarisel
classification_notes: Tarisel is a capitals-only display face rooted in Lombardic letterforms drawn from 13th-century Romanesque and Gothic inscriptions, with ornamental structural details that place it firmly in the display/decorative category rather than a conventional blackletter or serif classification.
summary: A Lombardic capitals display family by Martin Brendecke, drawn from 13th-century inscriptions at Amiens Cathedral and extended into two distinct subfamilies with variable weight axes.
description: Built entirely from Lombardic capitals, Tarisel takes its structural cues from medieval stonecutting at Amiens, then pushes the forms into contemporary display territory through elongated serifs and broken counterforms. The family splits into two branches: the Roman subfamily echoes Romanesque calligraphic origins, while Tarisel Gothic emphasises angular, compressed rhythm; multiple stylistic sets let the two lineages be mixed to produce hybrid settings. Ten static styles run from Light to Black across both subfamilies, with variable weight axes available as part of the full package, and the family was selected by the Type Directors Club in 2026.
foundry_blurb: Zetafonts describe Tarisel as a set of Lombardic capitals with a modern edge, inspired by 13th-century inscriptions from the cathedral of Amiens and conceived for massive display applications. They note that the design gives the term 'Gothic' new meaning through an innovative vocabulary of structural ornamentation, including elongated serifs and broken counterforms, and that multiple sets of stylistic alternates allow the user to highlight the Romanesque or Gothic roots of the letterforms, or to create a cryptic mixture of the two.

name: Biscottini
designer: Caffè Design, Cosimo Lorenzo Pancini, Mario De Libero
classification_notes: A display typeface built on a modular system of circular dots arranged on a humanist sans-serif skeleton; the dots swell and merge as weight increases, placing it firmly in the display category with a pixel-art and modular modernist flavour.
summary: A modular dot display family built on a humanist sans skeleton, where circular dots swell from thin strokes to bold blobs across seven weights and four decorative subfamilies.
description: Designed by Caffè Design with Zetafonts' Cosimo Lorenzo Pancini and Mario De Libero, Biscottini constructs its letterforms from circular dots that grow and merge as weight increases from Thin to ExtraBold. A variable font controls dot size and shape precisely, while four additional subfamilies swap the dots for stars (Sprinkle), square bites (BitBite), glazed blobs (Glaze) and soft fluid forms (Granny). The family covers 196 languages across Latin script and is aimed at food branding, wayfinding and motion graphics.
foundry_blurb: Zetafonts describe Biscottini as a typographic bakery extravaganza born from a collaboration between Caffè Design and the Zetafonts pastry team. Built on a modular system of circular dots following a humanist sans skeleton, the dots grow larger as weight increases until they touch and connect. Four decorative subfamilies extend the range with stars, square bites, glossy glazed shapes and soft whipped-cream forms, alongside a variable font that controls dot size and shape across two axes.

name: Crescendo
classification_notes: A condensed geometric sans with Art Deco proportions and extreme contrast between round and upright letterforms, placing it firmly in the geometric sans tradition with display-oriented sensibility.
summary: A condensed geometric sans by Cosimo Lorenzo Pancini drawing on Art Deco proportions and 1970s decorative sans traditions, designed as the serif-free counterpart to Ouverture.
description: Built around a condensed structure with sharply modulated geometry, Crescendo references the decorative sans serifs of the 1970s and the lettering sensibility of Herb Lubalin. The family spans three optical sizes — Display, Standard and Text — each in five weights from Light to Black, with a variable font available in the full package. Its 688-glyph set covers 200 languages and includes small capitals, oldstyle figures, fractions and stylistic alternates.
foundry_blurb: Zetafonts describe Crescendo as the sans-serif counterpart to their Ouverture family, translating its retro-futuristic character into clean lines and Art Deco-inspired proportions. They cite the decorative sans serifs of the 1970s and the spirit of Herb Lubalin as reference points, and note that the family's condensed structure, multiple optical sizes and variable font axes allow it to move from dramatic headlines and logotypes through to editorial and digital use.

name: Mira Confundo
classification_notes: Mira Confundo fuses textura blackletter modularity with chancery cursive and Roman capitals, making blackletter the dominant structural reference, though its hybridity and display intent set it apart from conventional Gothic classifications.
summary: A display typeface derived from folio 96 of Georg Bocksay's Mira Calligraphiae Monumenta, where diagonal fractures in the vertical strokes produce a saw-tooth rhythm that cuts across the Gothic flow.
description: Cosimo Lorenzo Pancini's study of Bocksay's 16th-century calligraphic model book resulted in a letterform system that fuses Roman capital proportions, textura-based lowercase structure and chancery cursive fluency into a single cohesive voice. The defining feature is the diagonal incision through vertical strokes, a device Pancini identified in a single folio that gives the typeface its oscillating visual pulse. With 346 glyphs, alternate uppercase and lowercase forms, lining and oldstyle figures, and decorative fleurons, the family is suited to editorial headlines, book covers and typographic poetry where historical strangeness is the point.
foundry_blurb: Zetafonts describe Mira Confundo as an experiment in readability and calligraphic excess, part of the Singularities set. They frame it as a fusion of three typographic logics — Roman capitals, textura modularity and chancery cursive — captured from Bocksay's fractured vertical strokes in the Mira Calligraphiae Monumenta. The name Confundo evokes not confusion but mixture and interweaving, inhabiting the tension between classical and calligraphic, Roman and Gothic, printed and written.

name: Aragorn
classification_notes: Aragorn roots itself in Old Style heritage with calligraphic rhythm and fluid, elongated forms. The Display subfamily amplifies high contrast and stylistic quirks, while the Text subfamily reduces contrast for extended reading, placing both firmly in the old style serif tradition.
summary: A 24-style Old Style serif by Francesco Canovaro spanning a Display subfamily with high contrast and calligraphic tension and a Text subfamily tuned for extended reading.
description: Designed at Zetafonts with Filippo Sgarlata and Andrea Tartarelli, Aragorn pairs fluid, elongated letterforms and subtle asymmetries with precise detailing across two optical subfamilies. The Display cuts push contrast and stylistic quirks for headlines, titles and brand work, while the Text cuts lower contrast and open the counters for long-form print or screen use. Variable fonts covering both weight and optical size axes are included in the full family package, giving continuous control across all 24 static styles.
foundry_blurb: Zetafonts describe Aragorn as a languid serif that bridges expressive display typography and editorial readability, with fluid elongated forms, Old Style heritage and high-contrast refinement in the Display subfamily, and a more restrained, low-contrast version optimised for extended reading in the Text subfamily. The full family comprises 24 styles with true italics, OpenType features including four stylistic sets, and variable fonts with weight and optical size axes covering 206 languages.

name: Television
designer: Francesco Canovaro, Cosimo Lorenzo Pancini, Mario De Libero
classification_notes: Explicitly described as a contemporary industrial grotesque, drawing on the geometric modular construction of Eurostile and Microgramma with low contrast, utilitarian sharpness and accentuated ink traps.
summary: An 84-style industrial grotesque spanning Condensed to Ultrawide, designed by Canovaro and Pancini as a futuristic extension of the Eurostile and Microgramma tradition.
description: Built on the modular, low-contrast skeleton of Aldo Novarese's Eurostile and Microgramma, Television stretches that visual logic across six width axes from Condensed to Ultrawide, each in seven weights with matching italics. Open-ended terminals and expanded geometric apertures replace tightly engineered solidity, giving the family a sense of forward motion suited to screen interfaces, titling and wide-format compositions. Accentuated ink traps sharpen performance at smaller sizes, and a variable font with weight, width and italic axes ships with the full family package.
foundry_blurb: Zetafonts describe Television as a contemporary industrial grotesque rooted in the mechanical clarity of Eurostile and Microgramma, but extended through a more expansive, futuristic lens. Amplifying horizontality across 84 styles, it forms an architectural typographic system with panoramic rhythm and cinematic presence, enriched with OpenType features including stylistic alternates, ligatures, positional numerals and extended stylistic sets.

name: Bimbo Pro
classification_notes: Bimbo Pro is a monoline script typeface with handwritten characteristics, drawing from the script tradition while incorporating display-oriented, hand-drawn qualities across its multiple weight variants.
summary: A monoline handwritten script by Francesco Canovaro, evolved from the original 2018 Bimbo family with added uppercase styles, variable font support and expanded Greek and Cyrillic coverage across 211 languages.
description: Originally derived from the Arsenale White typeface created with Italian illustrator Jonathan Calugi, Bimbo Pro extends the earlier Bimbo family with six pen-weight variants ranging from Ballpoint to Jumbo, each available in a standard and a dripping style. A variable font is included with the full family package. The monoline structure and open counters keep it legible at smaller sizes, making it workable beyond logo and title use for handwritten notes and informal typographic compositions.
foundry_blurb: Zetafonts describe Bimbo Pro as the evolved version of the original Bimbo family, itself a redesign of Arsenale White created with illustrator Jonathan Calugi. First released in 2018, the Pro version adds uppercase letters in both calligraphic and static styles, expands the character set with refined Greek and Cyrillic glyphs, and now supports over 90 languages. It is described as ideal for logos, minimal lettering and playful compositions, with OpenType features including automatic letter alternates, small capitals and stylistic sets.

name: Arrogante
designer: Andrea Tartarelli, Mario De Libero, Cosimo Lorenzo Pancini
classification_notes: Arrogante draws directly from the Bodoni and Didot tradition, using extreme stroke contrast, razor-sharp serifs and semi-condensed proportions in a manner clearly intended for display use rather than text setting.
summary: A Bodoni and Didot-derived display serif with extreme stroke contrast, semi-condensed proportions and overlapping serifs, released in 2024 by three Zetafonts designers.
description: Arrogante spans seven weights from Monoline to Bold, with a companion sub-family, Arrogantissimo, narrowing the proportions further for tighter headlines and posters. The high x-height and overlapping serifs push the design towards aggressive luxury display rather than text setting, and a variable font covering both weight and width axes is included in the full package. OpenType features include discretionary ligatures and nine stylistic sets, giving designers room to adjust specific letterforms.
foundry_blurb: Zetafonts describe Arrogante as a display family that embraces the self-assured grandeur of high-contrast serifs like Bodoni and Didot, pairing dramatic stroke contrast with semi-condensed proportions and razor-sharp serifs. Designed collaboratively by Andrea Tartarelli, Mario De Libero and Cosimo Lorenzo Pancini, the family covers 205 languages across 14 styles plus a variable font, with OpenType features including ligatures and stylistic alternates suited to luxury logos, editorial spreads and posters.

name: Bogart
classification_notes: A low-contrast oldstyle fat face drawing directly on Cooper Black, Goudy Heavy Face, Bookman and Windsor; the wide weight range and teardrop terminals at lighter weights place it firmly in the oldstyle display serif tradition.
summary: A wide-ranging oldstyle fat face by Francesco Canovaro that draws on Cooper Black, Goudy Heavy Face, Bookman and Windsor across nine weights, three widths and an alternate text-oriented subfamily.
description: Designed in 2020 and drawing explicitly on the goopy, low-contrast forms of Cooper Black and Goudy Heavy Face, the family traces its lineage through rub-on transfers and phototypesetting culture of the 1960s and 70s. The lighter weights pull teardrop terminals from Windsor rather than collapsing into shapeless blobs, giving the family genuine range from display titling to readable text. Over 1,600 glyphs per style cover 219 languages across Latin, Cyrillic and Greek, with swash forms, small caps, stylistic sets and variable font support rounding out the offering.
foundry_blurb: Zetafonts describe Bogart as a vibrant homage to iconic low-contrast oldstyle fat faces, blending the goopy charm of Bookman with the quirky elegance of Windsor. Designed by Francesco Canovaro with Andrea Tartarelli, it spans nine weights in Roman, Italic, Alt, Condensed and Compressed cuts, with an extended charset of over 1,600 glyphs and variable font support included in the full family package.

name: Maligna
classification_notes: A low-contrast serif with sharp, dimensionless vertices and rounded serif interlocks, designed to function across both display and text settings. The foundry positions it as a contemporary serif with display personality and text readability.
summary: A low-contrast contemporary serif by Valerio Monopoli, available in six weights with italics and variable fonts, distinguished by its signature truncated lowercase 'g' and floral-thorn serif details.
description: Designed by Rome-based Valerio Monopoli, the family spans six weights from Thin to Bold, each with a matching italic, plus variable upright and italic fonts. Low contrast and a 829-glyph character set covering 207 languages make it workable at small text sizes, while sharp, dimensionless vertices keep it effective at display scale. The signature uppercase 'R', the truncated lowercase 'g' that drops below the baseline, and the pointed serif details give it a recognisable character distinct from conventional text serifs.
foundry_blurb: Zetafonts describe Maligna as a mischievous contemporary serif that blends striking display personality with flawless text readability. The low contrast and extensive character set make it suitable for small text, while its dimensionless vertices remain sharp at any size. The typeface features characteristic serif shapes resembling floral appendices, thorns or spikes, with a signature uppercase 'R' and distinctive lowercase 'g' making it immediately recognisable.

name: Evans
classification_notes: The designers explicitly describe Evans as a semi-condensed old-style with a slight transitional slanted axis, combining classical old-style proportions with soft calligraphic detail and a mildly transitional stress angle.
summary: A semi-condensed old-style text family by Zetafonts, named after photographer Walker Evans and designed to maximise legibility in confined column widths.
description: Designed by Cosimo Lorenzo Pancini and Andrea Tartarelli, Evans pairs a classical old-style structure with a gently transitional axis and soft calligraphic stroke endings, resulting in a face that reads comfortably at small sizes in both print and digital contexts. The family runs to eight weights with true italics in both the standard and Narrow subfamilies, totalling 33 styles plus two variable fonts covering weight and width axes. Swash alternates, oldstyle figures and an Ornaments font extend its range for editorial, interface and identity work.
foundry_blurb: Zetafonts describe Evans as a text-oriented typeface inspired by the photojournalistic work of Walker Evans, designed with the ideals of simplicity, clarity and unpretentious honesty. The semi-condensed proportions were arrived at by gently narrowing classical letterforms to help designers fit longer text into tight spaces, while a slight transitional axis and soft calligraphic shapes keep reading smooth. The full family provides a Regular and a Narrow subfamily, each in eight weights with true italics, plus an Ornaments font and variable fonts covering weight and width.

name: Dialogue
classification_notes: Dialogue is explicitly rooted in garalde letterforms traceable to Francesco Griffo and 15th-century Venetian punchcutting, placing it firmly in the old-style/venetian serif tradition, with the added tension of brutalist sharpness applied to renaissance proportions.
summary: A garalde serif by Cosimo Lorenzo Pancini and Andrea Tartarelli that fuses Venetian renaissance proportions with sharper, more contemporary detailing across two distinct stylistic subfamilies.
description: Built on the old-style letterform tradition of Francesco Griffo and 15th-century Venice, the typeface applies crisp edges and deliberate structural tension alongside the high readability characteristic of humanist serifs. Two subfamilies, Dialogue A and Dialogue B, offer contrasting interpretations of the same formal vocabulary, with six weights and matching italics in each. Non-standard punctuation and conversation-specific symbols are accessible via OpenType features, and the full family includes variable font files for both upright and italic axes.
foundry_blurb: Zetafonts describe Dialogue as emerging from a mutual exchange of ideas about traditional garalde letterforms tracing back to Venice and Francesco Griffo. Designed by Cosimo Lorenzo Pancini and Andrea Tartarelli, it draws on brutalist design principles alongside a sense of balance, aiming to work at both text and display sizes. The result, they say, is elegant yet rugged, connecting a pixel-perfect contemporary aesthetic with renaissance proportions and introducing bold design details intended to draw the reader in.

name: Heading Now
designer: Francesco Canovaro, Cosimo Lorenzo Pancini, Andrea Tartarelli, Mario De Libero
classification_notes: Described explicitly as a grotesque sans; the narrow widths exhibit an industrial quality with straight-sided letterforms, while wider widths follow more traditional rounded grotesque forms.
summary: A grotesque sans super-family of 176 static styles plus two variable fonts, built across 11 widths and 8 weights, with an emphasis on ultra-narrow industrial letterforms that shift toward traditional rounded grotesque proportions as widths expand.
description: Designed by Francesco Canovaro and developed further by Cosimo Lorenzo Pancini and Andrea Tartarelli, the family spans from tightly compressed, straight-sided widths suited to headers and wayfinding, through to wider display and body text cuts with matching italics. Over 1,100 characters cover 200+ languages in Latin, Cyrillic and Greek, and a full suite of OpenType features includes small caps, old-style figures, high-legibility alternates and squared punctuation. Variable font versions of both upright and italic are available with the full family package.
foundry_blurb: Zetafonts describe Heading Now as a grotesque sans super-family built around 11 different widths, with particular emphasis on ultra-narrow weights that create a distinctive vertical texture through carefully balanced inner and outer glyph spaces. They note that the industrial grotesque character of the narrow widths gives way to more rounded, traditional grotesque forms as proportions widen, and that the family is suited to headers, infographics, wayfinding, logo design and timetables, as well as body text in the medium widths.

name: Unigeo
classification_notes: A constructed geometric sans-serif built on modular principles, referencing photo-lettering era letterforms and vintage computing aesthetics. Three subfamilies vary the level of period-specific detailing and decorative treatment.
summary: A geometric sans family in three subfamilies, each drawing from vintage computing and photo-lettering era references at different levels of display flair and decorative intensity.
description: Designed by Cosimo Lorenzo Pancini with Francesco Canovaro, Unigeo spans three subfamilies named after computing memory sizes: Unigeo32 favours contemporary shapes optimised for text use, Unigeo64 is the display-oriented variant with tight tracking and period-specific letterforms, and Unigeo128 applies an optical-art stripe treatment to the same skeleton. All three are available in eight weights from Thin to Extrabold, with italics on the 32 and 64 variants, variable font versions for each, and coverage across Latin, Greek and Cyrillic for 210 languages.
foundry_blurb: Zetafonts describe Unigeo as an eulogy to vintage computing design, with its geometric modularity, ultra-tight tracking and striped rainbow overload. The family gives a new perspective on the geometric sans genre by adding a vintage flair to selected letters while keeping optical adjustments to a minimum, prioritising the modular, constructed look. The three memory-version subfamilies each offer a different relationship with the reference material, from the workhorse text cuts of Unigeo32 to the retro display orientation of Unigeo64 and the op-art stripes of Unigeo128.

name: Barnum
designer: Cosimo Lorenzo Pancini, Mario De Libero
classification_notes: Barnum is a Clarendon-model slab serif, rooted in the bracketed, high-contrast slab serif tradition originating with Thorowgood and Besley in 1845, with deliberate departures including serifless variants on key characters.
summary: A Clarendon-model slab serif by Cosimo Lorenzo Pancini that adds serifless treatment on select characters and a variable weight axis across 48 styles including Condensed and Compressed.
description: Drawing directly from the bracketed slab serifs of Thorowgood and Besley's 1845 Clarendon, Barnum runs eight weights from Book to Ultra across normal, condensed and compressed widths, each with matching italics. Lighter cuts are drawn with transitional proportions suited to body text, while heavier cuts shift toward display work with more commanding stroke contrast. The serifless treatment on C, G and S gives the family a modest brutalist inflection that distinguishes it from straightforward historical revivals.
foundry_blurb: Zetafonts describe Barnum as an homage to Clarendon's historical significance, designed by Cosimo Lorenzo Pancini with Mario De Libero. They highlight serifless versions of key characters as a contemporary and brutalist departure, a dynamic weight axis that adapts letterform proportions from display to text use, and coverage of 198 languages across Latin and Greek. The family ships in 48 static styles plus two variable fonts covering weight and width axes.

name: Artusi
classification_notes: Described explicitly as a transitional serif by the foundry, with high contrast between thicks and thins in lighter weights and calligraphic details in heavier weights; the Grande subfamily adds a distinct display optical size.
summary: A transitional serif by Francesco Canovaro, drawing on the legacy of Italian food writer Pellegrino Artusi across seven weights and two optical sizes.
description: Designed at Zetafonts and released in 2022, the family spans text and display versions under the Roman and Grande subfamilies, with 28 styles plus two variable fonts covering weight and optical size axes. Lighter weights lean on high contrast and spiky serifs suited to text setting, while heavier cuts carry calligraphic swashes and luscious curves aimed at packaging and logotypes. An extensive OpenType feature set includes swashes, oldstyle figures, discretionary ligatures and multiple stylistic sets, with coverage across 208 languages.
foundry_blurb: Zetafonts describe Artusi as a typographic homage to Italian traditional cuisine, named after celebrated food writer Pellegrino Artusi. Designed by Francesco Canovaro with Mario De Libero and visual design by Sofia Bandini, the typeface frames each weight as a different course in a meal, from delicate Extralight starters to opulent Heavy desserts, combining traditional Italian old-style forms with contemporary refinement and a range of OpenType features including swashes, alternates and ligatures.

name: Codec Pro ME
designer: Cosimo Lorenzo Pancini, Francesco Canovaro, Andrea Tartarelli, Oded Ezer, Omaima Dajani
classification_notes: Codec Pro ME is a geometric sans-serif extending the 2017 Codec Pro design with Arabic and Hebrew scripts; its construction follows the modernist geometric tradition adapted carefully for non-Latin writing systems.
summary: A Middle Eastern extension of Codec Pro covering Arabic and Hebrew scripts across eleven weights, developed with specialist collaborators Oded Ezer and Omaima Dajani.
description: Built on the geometric sans foundation of Codec Pro, first drawn in 2017 by Cosimo Lorenzo Pancini and Francesco Canovaro, this extension adds Arabic and Hebrew coverage across 1718 glyphs and 217 languages. The Hebrew was designed by Oded Ezer and the Arabic developed in consultation with Omaima Dajani, with the team drawing on advice from Pascal Zoghbi of 29LT to avoid imposing Latin structural logic onto non-Latin scripts. Eleven weights from Thin to Fat are available as static fonts and as a variable weight axis.
foundry_blurb: Zetafonts describe Codec Pro ME as an extended version of their Codec Pro typeface providing full Arabic and Hebrew script support, developed in collaboration with Oded Ezer and Omaima Dajani. They emphasise the care taken to study Arabic typographic conventions rather than simply transposing Latin modernist rules, and cite the family's role in bridging cultures through inclusive typographic design. The release received a Communication Arts Typeface Design award in 2024.

name: Ambra Sans
designer: Francesco Canovaro, Cosimo Lorenzo Pancini, Andrea Tartarelli
classification_notes: Zetafonts classify Ambra as a humanist sans, drawn from a calligraphic skeleton with low contrast and a tall x-height. Its origins in the handmade letterform tradition place it firmly in the humanist sans subcategory.
summary: A humanist sans by Canovaro and Pancini that reinterprets Tartarelli's Tarif, balancing a calligraphic skeleton against a post-digital low-contrast execution across 30 styles.
description: Built from a development of Andrea Tartarelli's Tarif, Ambra splits into two subfamilies: a display cut with a taller x-height and single-storey g, and a text cut with slightly reduced proportions and untapered stems for better small-size screen rendering. Eight weights run from Light to Heavy in the display subfamily, with seven in the text subfamily, each accompanied by true italics and a variable font covering weight, optical size and italic axes. Coverage extends to 205 languages across Latin and Greek scripts.
foundry_blurb: Zetafonts describe Ambra as a humanist sans drawn around a lively, expressive skeleton with a contemporary post-digital sensibility implying low contrast and a tall x-height. They frame the design as a search for the natural signature of handmade humanist letterforms, calling humanist design the ghost in the machine of the font, aimed at providing branding and editorial-focused designers with a soft, welcoming alternative to the rigid formal tone of most sans serifs.

name: Keratine
classification_notes: Keratine sits at the intersection of Fraktur and Humanist antiqua traditions, filtered through a quasi-pixelated, digitally carved aesthetic. Its blackletter classification reflects the Fraktur gestural energy and Gothic letterforms at its core, even as it borrows humanistic proportions.
summary: A hybrid of Fraktur and Humanist antiqua filtered through low-resolution digital aesthetics, designed by Cosimo Lorenzo Pancini and released in 2022.
description: Drawing on the obscure historical middleground between blackletter and Roman type — exemplified by the Pannartz-Sweynheym Subiaco types of 1462 — Keratine applies a quasi-pixelated, digitally carved treatment to letterforms that carry both Fraktur energy and Humanist proportion. Eight weights with matching italics allow the family to move from a contrasted, text-oriented light end to a more expressive display range, with a variable font axis reinforcing that spectrum. OpenType alternates including Gothic letterforms, single-storey variants and medieval forms extend its expressive range considerably. Awarded by both the Type Directors Club and Communication Arts in 2023.
foundry_blurb: Zetafonts describe Keratine as an exploration of the territory between antiqua and blackletter — not as historical research, but as a way to recover an unexpected contemporary dynamism. Designed by Cosimo Lorenzo Pancini with development contributions from Mario De Libero, Andrea Tartarelli and Francesco Canovaro, it uses digital aesthetics to merge the proportions of humanistic type with the gestural energy of Fraktur, yielding a digitally carved, quasi-pixelated appearance that balances small-size readability with display-size personality across 16 styles and a variable font.

name: Bakemono
classification_notes: A sans-serif with a geometric underpinning drawn from typewriter technology and eastern brush calligraphy, with monowidth proportions forming the conceptual spine across all three subfamilies.
summary: A 42-style superfamily by Francesco Canovaro built around the tension between fixed and proportional widths, drawing on typewriter mechanics and eastern brush calligraphy.
description: The family runs across three subfamilies: Bakemono Text for proportional reading, Bakemono Mono for true monospaced setting, and Bakemono Stereo as an intermediate monowidth variant that allows weight changes without disrupting text alignment. Seven weights from Thin to Extrabold with italics throughout, two variable axes and 948 glyphs covering Latin, Greek and Cyrillic across 218 languages. Shortlisted at D&AD 2022 and awarded at PRINT and Indigo in 2023, with Arabic and CJK extensions planned.
foundry_blurb: Zetafonts describe Bakemono as an exploration of the duality between fixed and proportional width, with designer Francesco Canovaro mixing the principles of mechanical typewriter technology with the fixed-grid traditions of eastern brush calligraphy. The name comes from the shape-shifting yokai of Japanese folklore, reflecting the superfamily's metamorphic nature across proportional, monowidth and monospaced subfamilies. Extended Latin and Cyrillic coverage is included, with Bakemono Arabic and Bakemono Kana in development.

name: Marcovaldo
classification_notes: A condensed high-contrast wedge serif with roots in the Elzevir and DeVinne traditions, optimised for display use. The pronounced contrast and wedge-shaped serifs place it firmly within the Latin display serif category.
summary: A condensed wedge serif in seven weights, drawn by Andrea Tartarelli as an extension of the Calvino family and shaped by the Elzevir and DeVinne traditions.
description: Developed as part of Zetafonts' Calvino typographic world, Marcovaldo draws on the high-contrast condensed rhythm of Elzevir and DeVinne printing types. Lighter weights carry tight spacing that evokes vintage editorial setting, while bolder weights open up for a more relaxed texture suited to headlines and book covers. The family includes a variable Roman weight axis and covers 200 languages across 420 glyphs.
foundry_blurb: Zetafonts describe Marcovaldo as a condensed wedge serif family inspired by the theme of Visibility from Italo Calvino's Six Memos for the Next Millennium, designed by Andrea Tartarelli as an evolution of the Calvino family. They position it as blending old-style letterform sharpness with modern digital aesthetics, suited to editorial headlines, book covers and display settings. The family won a Paris Design Award for Typeface Design in 2022.

name: Calvino
classification_notes: Tartarelli explicitly references sixteenth-century garalde typography as the structural model, with calligraphic hand influence adding rhythm and warmth to the letterforms.
summary: A garalde serif by Andrea Tartarelli structured around Italo Calvino's literary principles of exactitude, lightness and multiplicity, spanning text and display subfamilies across 34 styles.
description: The design draws on sixteenth-century garalde models for its controlled, legible architecture, then tempers that rigour with calligraphic influence in the tradition of Gudrun Zapf. A display subfamily, Calvino Grande, shares the same formal structure but adopts condensed proportions, sharper details and tighter metrics for headlines. The full family covers over 200 languages in Latin and Cyrillic, includes four variable fonts with weight and optical size axes, and ships with an extensive set of OpenType features.
foundry_blurb: Zetafonts describe Calvino as a typeface family built around the six principles Italo Calvino outlined in Six Memos for the Next Millennium. Exactitude and visibility come from garalde references; lightness and quickness from calligraphic influence following Gudrun Zapf. Multiplicity is expressed through a wide weight range and the addition of Calvino Grande, a condensed display subfamily with sharper details. The full 34-style family covers Latin and Cyrillic across 206 languages, with variable font support for fine-tuning weight and optical size.

name: Asgard
classification_notes: Built on a geometric sans skeleton with high-contrast details drawn from wood-type and letterpress vernacular traditions, giving it an unusual tension between rational structure and expressive weight contrast.
summary: A 72-style geometric sans by Francesco Canovaro that borrows high-contrast details from wood-type and letterpress traditions across three widths and a backslant axis.
description: Designed by Francesco Canovaro with contributions from Mario De Libero and Andrea Tartarelli, the family spans Fit, Regular and Wide widths at eight weights from Thin to Fat, each with roman, italic and backslant cuts. The 968-glyph set covers over 200 languages across Latin, Greek and Cyrillic, and all three variation axes are accessible via an included variable font. The combination of a rational geometric skeleton with calligraphy-influenced stroke contrast gives it a range unusual for a display sans.
foundry_blurb: Zetafonts describe Asgard as Francesco Canovaro's attempt to merge the raw energy of extra-bold sans-serif typography with the expressivity of high-contrast, calligraphy-inspired letterforms. The name references the mythical home of the Norse gods, evoking power and strength alongside delicate beauty. Extended by Tartarelli and De Libero into a 72-font super-family with a variable font version, the design covers three widths, eight weights and a slanted axis, with full OpenType features throughout.

name: Arsenica
designer: Francesco Canovaro, Debora Manetti, Mario De Libero, Andrea Tartarelli, Cosimo Lorenzo Pancini
classification_notes: Rooted in Italian poster lettering of the early twentieth century, Arsenica draws on Old Style proportions with Art Nouveau and Deco inflections, ranging from low-contrast antiqua cuts to high-contrast display weights across four distinct subfamilies.
summary: A superfamily of 41 styles drawing on early twentieth-century Italian poster lettering, built across four subfamilies from low-contrast antiqua to high-contrast display and body text cuts.
description: Designed by Francesco Canovaro and a team at Zetafonts, Arsenica traces its lineage to the eclectic lettering of artists like Giorgio Muggiani, whose work for Cinzano and Pirelli blended Old Style structure with Art Nouveau ornament. The family spans four distinct subfamilies: Antiqua preserves wide proportions and quirky period details, the core Display branch pushes contrast and calligraphic weight range, Alt strips away the decorative flourishes for a more restrained modern serif, and Text recalibrates contrast and spacing for sustained body copy. Two variable fonts covering weight and optical size axes, over 900 glyphs per style, and support for more than 200 languages across Latin, Greek, Cyrillic and Arabic make it a genuinely broad design system.
foundry_blurb: Zetafonts describe Arsenica as inspired by Italian poster design at the turn of the twentieth century, when typography, lettering and illustration were closely interwoven. They explain that dawning nationalist movements favoured traditional Old Style letterforms inflected with Art Nouveau and Deco sensibility, as seen in the work of Giorgio Muggiani. The full family of 41 weights includes two variable fonts, over 900 glyphs, coverage of more than 200 languages across Latin, Greek, Cyrillic and Arabic, and a rich set of OpenType features including stylistic sets, discretionary ligatures, contextual alternates and positional figures.

name: Coco Sharp
classification_notes: Built on a geometric sans skeleton in the tradition of Futura, but with humanist proportions applied to key letterforms. Sharp terminals distinguish it from the rounded Coco Gothic and Cocogoose predecessors.
summary: A geometric sans with humanist corrections and five graded x-height sub-families, evolved from the Coco typographic project begun in 2013.
description: Developed by Cosimo Lorenzo Pancini with Francesco Canovaro and Andrea Tartarelli, Coco Sharp strips the rounded corners from its predecessors Coco Gothic and Cocogoose to produce a version equally suited to display headlines and running text. Five sub-families graded from XS to XL give designers direct control over x-height and readability across different point sizes, and variable font versions cover both weight and optical-size axes simultaneously. Over 2,000 glyphs support Latin, Greek and Cyrillic across more than 200 languages, with small capitals, positional figures and alternate letterforms via OpenType.
foundry_blurb: Zetafonts describe Coco Sharp as the newest evolution of the Coco typographic project, first developed in 2013, drawing on vernacular grotesque sign-painting, modernist ideals and the aesthetic of Coco Chanel. The design uses a geometric sans skeleton with humanist proportions and optical corrections to produce a warmer, subtly vintage texture. The five graded x-height sub-families and variable font versions are presented as tools for fine-grained control of text readability and spatial economy across both display and body-text settings.

name: Eastman
designer: Francesco Canovaro, Andrea Tartarelli, Cosimo Lorenzo Pancini, Solenn Bordeau
classification_notes: Monolinear strokes, geometric construction influenced by Bauhaus ideals, with a nod to 19th-century grotesque proportions across four widths including a humanist-proportioned Grotesque subfamiliy.
summary: A large geometric sans family from Zetafonts spanning four widths, ten weights, and 176 styles, with Bauhaus-inflected construction and extensive alternate character sets.
description: Designed by Francesco Canovaro and Andrea Tartarelli in 2020, Eastman pairs monolinear strokes and a tall x-height with a weight range running from Thin to Heavy across Roman, Grotesque, Condensed and Compressed widths. Each width ships with a standard and an Alt cut, the latter pre-loading unusual alternate letterforms suited to display and logo work without needing to dig through the glyphs palette. Variable font versions covering weight, width and italic axes are available in the full package, and script coverage extends to Latin, Cyrillic and Greek across 219 languages.
foundry_blurb: Zetafonts describe Eastman as a geometric sans workhorse conceived for maximum versatility in both display and text, influenced by Bauhaus ideals and the pragmatic character of 19th-century grotesques. They highlight its tall x-height for on-screen and print readability, a broad set of OpenType features including small caps and positional figures, and an extensive range of alternate characters and stylistic sets designed to support editorial and branding work.

name: Malik
classification_notes: Malik uses flared serifs that emerge gradually from stems and end at a sharp angle, bridging Old Style Roman letterforms with contemporary sans readability — a distinct subcategory sometimes called 'lapidary' or 'flared sans'.
summary: A flared sans by Andrea Tartarelli that bridges Old Style Roman structure with contemporary sans readability through stems that taper to sharp angles rather than conventional serifs.
description: Designed in 2020 by Andrea Tartarelli, Malik draws on the Simoncini Method and the calligraphic sensibility of Frederic Goudy and Ed Benguiat, producing details such as a reverse-contrast uppercase B and a calligraphic lowercase k. The family spans eight weights from Extralight to Heavy, all with true italics, plus a Book weight for fine-tuning text density and a Heavy cut with deep inktraps suited to logo work. Variable fonts for both upright and italic, small capitals, old-style figures and Cyrillic support across 218 languages round out a well-equipped 16-style family.
foundry_blurb: Zetafonts describe Malik as a flared sans serif named after the Arabic word for 'king', designed to merge the classical letterforms of Roman Old Style typefaces with the readability of modern sans. The family is noted for its calligraphic influences, its striking Heavy Display weight with deep inktraps, and extensive OpenType feature support including small capitals, stylistic sets and positional numerals, with coverage of over 1,000 Latin and Cyrillic characters across 218 languages.

name: Blacker Sans Pro
classification_notes: Described by the foundry as monolinear and grotesque-inspired, with varying contrast levels across three subfamilies; the Display subfamily introduces high-contrast stroke variation atypical of pure grotesques but the overall skeleton and approach remains within that tradition.
summary: A 60-style grotesque sans designed as a companion to Blacker Pro, split across three subfamilies with varying stroke contrast from monolinear to high-contrast display.
description: Built as a serif-less counterpart to the Blacker family, this family retains the dark, elegant character of its predecessor whilst replacing wedge serifs with a monolinear grotesque structure. Three subfamilies — Classic, Text and Display — apply different degrees of contrast to the same skeleton, from readability-optimised Text to the high-contrast curves of Display. The full family spans 60 static styles plus six variable fonts, with over 1,600 characters per style covering 220-plus languages across Latin, Cyrillic and Greek.
foundry_blurb: Zetafonts describe Blacker Sans Pro as a complete redesign of the original 2019 family, created as a sans-serif companion to Blacker by Cosimo Lorenzo Pancini and Andrea Tartarelli. The family was developed to retain Blacker's dark elegance whilst adopting a more even, monolinear approach; two optical subfamilies, Text and Display, were added to cover use cases the original monolinear cut could not, with Display offering extreme contrast between thin curves and thick strokes. OpenType features including small caps, swashes, ligatures and alternate forms are available across all 60 weights.

name: Codec Pro
designer: Francesco Canovaro, Cosimo Lorenzo Pancini, Andrea Tartarelli
classification_notes: Codec Pro is a geometric sans-serif with two core moods: Codec Cold, which uses terminals cut parallel or perpendicular to the baseline for a constructed geometric look, and Codec Warm, which uses open diagonal cuts and humanist shapes for a softer feel.
summary: An eleven-weight geometric sans with built-in stylistic sets that toggle between a cold, constructed look and a warmer, more humanist one.
description: Originally developed in 2017 and relaunched as Codec Pro, this family spans eleven weights from Thin to Fat, each with an italic, plus two variable font files. Stylistic sets distinguish Codec Cold, with terminals parallel or perpendicular to the baseline, from Codec Warm, which uses open diagonal cuts and softer forms, while logo variants add ligatures and horizontally stretched uppercase characters. Script support covers Latin, Greek and Cyrillic across 214 languages with 1,446 glyphs per style.
foundry_blurb: Zetafonts describe Codec Pro as a redesigned and expanded version of their original Codec typeface, developed as a research into the subtleties and variations of geometric sans-serif design. They frame it as a swiss-knife for designers, offering a multi-voice type system that ranges from the geometric precision of Codec Cold to the humanist warmth of Codec Warm, with expressive logo variants alongside broad multilingual support.

name: Blacker Pro
classification_notes: Blacker Pro draws explicitly on Didone contrast with wedge-shaped serifs and high stroke variation across optical sizes, placing it firmly in the modern serif / Didone tradition rather than transitional or humanist categories.
summary: A Didone-influenced editorial serif with three optical subfamilies, nine width and size variants, and variable font support across 221 languages.
description: Designed by Cosimo Lorenzo Pancini and Andrea Tartarelli, Blacker Pro pairs wedge-shaped serifs with pronounced Didone stroke contrast across Text, Subhead and Display optical sizes, each available in standard, Narrow and Condensed widths. A weight range from Light to Fat, plus a 2024-introduced Subhead subfamily bridging the contrast gap between Text and Display, gives the family unusual coverage for editorial and advertising work. Variable fonts with weight and optical size axes are included in the full family package.
foundry_blurb: Zetafonts describe Blacker Pro as a modern serif family designed for editorial applications, built around Didone-inspired contrast, open counters, a tall x-height and short descenders. The family spans three optical subfamilies along a variable axis, with a weight range running from light to ultra-heavy fat styles, a width axis from roman to condensed, and support for over 200 languages across Latin, Cyrillic and Greek alphabets.

name: Lovelace
designer: Cosimo Lorenzo Pancini, Andrea Tartarelli, Mariachiara Fantini
classification_notes: Described by Zetafonts as a revival of nineteenth-century Old Style typography, drawing on Renaissance calligraphic letterforms and Elzevir revival styles, with calligraphic curves and angled serifs in the tradition of Phemister and Alexander Kay.
summary: A 30-style Victorian Old Style revival in two optical sizes plus a connected script, named after Ada Lovelace and drawing on Phemister, Elzevir and calligraphic traditions.
description: Designed by Cosimo Lorenzo Pancini, Andrea Tartarelli and Mariachiara Fantini, it revives nineteenth-century Old Style letterforms with calligraphic curves and angled serifs, consciously stepping away from the rigidity of Bodonian models. Two optical sizes split the workload: the Text subfamily prioritises generous x-height and screen legibility, while the Display cuts favour high contrast and fine detailing suited to editorial headlines. A separate Script subfamily with swashes and connected lowercase adds a third register, and all 30 styles cover 208 languages across Latin, Greek and Cyrillic.
foundry_blurb: Zetafonts describe Lovelace as a homage to nineteenth-century Old Style typography, taking inspiration from the calligraphic letter shapes of Phemister and Alexander Kay, whose influence reached Benguiat and Goudy in the 1970s, while also drawing on the Elzevir revival tradition. Named after the algorist Ada Lovelace, the family spans Display and Text optical sizes across six weights from Extralight to Extrabold, complemented by a display script with swashes designed by an in-house calligrapher. OpenType features include small caps, stylistic alternates, old-style figures, ligatures and case-sensitive forms.

name: Klein
designer: Francesco Canovaro, Andrea Tartarelli, Cosimo Lorenzo Pancini
classification_notes: Rooted in the geometric sans tradition via Futura, but tempered with humanist and grotesque details that soften the strict modernist geometry, bringing it closer to Gill Sans than pure Renner.
summary: A 54-font superfamily by Zetafonts that reinterprets Futura's geometry with humanist and grotesque softening across display, text and condensed subfamilies.
description: Designed by Francesco Canovaro and Andrea Tartarelli with Cosimo Lorenzo Pancini, Klein takes Paul Renner's proportions as its starting point and departs from strict modernist purity with details drawn from humanist sans serifs, particularly Gill Sans. The family spans nine weights with matching italics, repeated across a standard display cut, a Text version with a raised x-height for small-size legibility, and a Condensed subfamily for space-constrained editorial and advertising work. Script coverage extends to Latin, Greek and Cyrillic across 218 languages, and a variable font is available in the full package.
foundry_blurb: Zetafonts describe Klein as a love letter to Futura, starting from Paul Renner's iconic letterforms and proportions before departing with humanist touches and grotesque solutions, some evoking the softness of Gill Sans. The result is a workhorse superfamily of 54 fonts with multilingual coverage of over 200 languages. Named as both a nod to functionality and a homage to French Nouveau Réaliste artist Yves Klein, the family includes display, text and condensed subfamilies alongside a variable font.

name: Extenda
designer: Francesco Canovaro, Andrea Tartarelli, Cosimo Lorenzo Pancini, Mario De Libero
classification_notes: A sans-serif designed explicitly for display use, with its primary axis being width rather than weight, spanning eleven steps from ultra-condensed to ultra-wide. The Extendable variant and XS subfamily reinforce its display-first, experimental character.
summary: A width-axis sans-serif spanning eleven steps from ultra-condensed to ultra-wide, built for display headlines and logo work across Latin, Cyrillic and Greek.
description: Rather than varying weight, Extenda organises its 67-plus styles along a width axis, running from the hairline-thin Pica through to the sprawling Yotta, with italic and backslant cuts at each step. The XS subfamily introduces wider counter spaces and open tracking for improved legibility at the compact end, while the Extendable style uses OpenType logic to increment each letter's width automatically, producing a dynamic visual rhythm. Variable font support, small caps, old-style figures and multiple stylistic sets round out a family aimed squarely at editorial display and identity work.
foundry_blurb: Zetafonts describe Extenda as a powerful and flexible tool for strong headlines, logos and display text with tight spacing and maximum space coverage. Rather than offering weight variants, it provides a fine-grained range of widths for control in editorial display use and proportional size variation in logo design. The XS subfamily retains Extenda's compact texture while enlarging counter spaces for improved readability, and the unique Extendable weight uses OpenType scripting to dynamically widen or narrow each successive letter.

name: Cocogoose Pro
designer: Cosimo Lorenzo Pancini, Francesco Canovaro, Andrea Tartarelli, Mario De Libero
classification_notes: Built on a geometric sans skeleton with rounded corners and very low stroke contrast, drawing on both modernist geometry and vernacular sign-painting traditions. Its tall x-height and bold colour push it firmly into display territory.
summary: A rounded geometric sans drawn on a sign-painting vernacular, expanded across five widths, seven weights, layerable display variants and variable font technology.
description: Originally designed by Cosimo Lorenzo Pancini in 2013 and substantially overhauled in 2020, the family now spans 85-plus styles across Standard, Narrow, Condensed, Compressed and Ultracompressed widths. Decorative variants include Inline, Outlined, Block Border, Block Shadow and a Letterpress cut with programmed letter randomisation for an aged-print effect. Coverage extends to over 200 languages across Latin, Cyrillic and Greek, with nine stylistic sets, small capitals and a dedicated Text subfamily for body use.
foundry_blurb: Zetafonts describe Cocogoose Pro as influenced by vernacular sign-painting and modernist ideals, built on a geometric sans skeleton softened by rounded corners. They cite its low contrast, dark colour and tall x-height as making it suitable for logos, headings and vintage-inspired branding, while a 2020 redesign added new widths, a darkmode weight range, variable font versions and a text subfamily for improved screen and print readability.

name: Coco Gothic
classification_notes: Draws directly from early geometric sans models such as Futura and Berthold Grotesque, with slight corner rounding and humanist proportional touches that soften the pure geometric framework.
summary: A geometric sans by Cosimo Lorenzo Pancini referencing early twentieth-century typefaces such as Futura, with slight corner rounding meant to evoke the warmth of letterpress ink spread.
description: Designed in response to a biography of Coco Chanel, the typeface pairs geometric sans structure with humanist proportional adjustments and subtly rounded corners to recall the softening effect of ink on paper. The Pro revision, released six years after the original, expands the family to three subfamilies — standard, Alternate and Display — each in seven weights with matching italics and variable font versions. Ten alternate historical sets covering styles from art deco to 1980s minimalism are accessible via OpenType, and the full family supports 216 languages across Latin, Greek and Cyrillic.
foundry_blurb: Zetafonts describe Coco Gothic Pro as a complete redraw and expansion of the original 2015 family, inspired by Cosimo Lorenzo Pancini's reading of a Coco Chanel biography. The corner rounding is a deliberate digital translation of ink spread on paper, and the inclusion of ten historical alternate sets is positioned as an encyclopedia of twentieth-century styles. The full family includes six variable font versions, among them the Coco Gothic Tardis variable, which exposes historical versions through variable sliders.

name: Ouverture
classification_notes: A condensed Didone with high contrast and ultra-compressed proportions across optical sizes, drawing on classical modern serif tradition while incorporating idiosyncratic details reminiscent of University Roman.
summary: A condensed Didone designed for Lucca Comics & Games 2024, drawing on late-90s anime and gaming aesthetics to reframe classical high-contrast serif forms with a retro-futuristic character.
description: Designed by Cosimo Lorenzo Pancini with Andrea Tartarelli, the family spans four optical subfamilies — Text, Deck, Display and Grande — each tuned for different scales and media. The ultra-compressed proportions and sharp contrast of the display cuts give way to more refined, readable forms at text sizes, while details echo University Roman's idiosyncratic playfulness. The family has a sans-serif counterpart, Crescendo, which translates the same retro-futuristic energy into geometric, Art Deco-inflected forms.
foundry_blurb: Zetafonts describe Ouverture as a shape-shifting condensed Didone inspired by late-90s video games and Japanese anime, and their postmodern reinvention of stylised old-style serifs. Originally created as a custom typeface for the pop-culture identity of Lucca Comics & Games 2024 — a festival dedicated to the Fantastic Worlds of Yoshitaka Amano — it bridges classical typographic heritage with the dynamic energy of gaming and anime, and is suited for editorial, branding and advertising use across print and digital.

name: Blacker Mono
classification_notes: A monospaced typeface with pronounced bracketed serifs derived from the Blacker Pro display serif family, sitting firmly in the mono category while retaining strong serif character. Its extreme display weights and optical sizes push it towards editorial rather than purely utilitarian coding use.
summary: A monospaced serif drawn from the Blacker Pro family, transplanting its sharp 'evil serif' aesthetic into a typewriter-style format with three optical sizes and extreme display weights.
description: Developed for the 2022 editorial project A Beautiful Mistake by OFFF TLV, it adapts the Blacker Pro letterforms for fixed-width use by modifying only the serifs rather than distorting the underlying skeletons, producing an uneven rhythm unusual in monospaced type. Nine static styles run from Light to Heavy, with two alternate roman cuts and a variable font covering both weight and optical size axes. The full OpenType feature set carries over from Blacker Pro, including small caps, positional numerals and stylistic alternates, making it as well-equipped for complex editorial layouts as for code environments.
foundry_blurb: Zetafonts describe Blacker Mono as a monospaced adaptation of their Blacker Pro typeface, bringing its distinctive 'evil serif' aesthetics into the world of typewriter and coding fonts. Rather than distorting letterforms to achieve uniform spacing, the designers retained the original Blacker Pro skeletons and adjusted only the serifs, resulting in an unexpected, uneven rhythm. They highlight three optical sizes and extreme display weights as rare features for a monospaced family, and note the inclusion of the full OpenType feature set shared across all Blacker variants.

name: Arsenica Arabic
classification_notes: A Naskh-rooted Arabic typeface with Old Style contrast logic, drawing on translation-model stroke contrast with sharp, acute joins rather than the soft terminals of classical digitised Naskh revivals. The Latin component follows the same expansion-based drawing philosophy as the broader Arsenica superfamily.
summary: A Naskh-rooted Arabic type system by Francesco Canovaro and Murad Alshawareb, designed to pair with the Latin Arsenica superfamily across brand and editorial work.
description: Built on a traditional Naskh skeleton but executed with sharp, triangular joins and high contrast drawn from a translation model rather than classical calligraphy, the family splits into two subfamilies: Arsenica Arabic Antiqua, which rounds terminals for warmth in editorial and branding contexts, and the main family, which pushes contrast to its sharpest for poster and logo use. Seven weights run across both subfamilies, with a variable font included in the full package covering weight and optical size axes. The design was developed with guidance from Nadine Chahine and completes a multilingual system alongside the Latin, Greek and Cyrillic Arsenica families.
foundry_blurb: Zetafonts describe Arsenica Arabic as a Naskh-rooted typeface designed to find a genuine typographic parallel to the Latin Arsenica Superfamily, sharing what they call an eclectic tension between tradition and modernity. The design avoids classical calligraphic conventions in favour of contemporary type design logic, with two subfamilies — Antiqua for warmth and the main family for maximum visual impact — completing a multilingual system for brand identity and cultural publishing.

name: Milligram Arabic
classification_notes: A sans-serif rooted in Naskh script traditions, prioritising texture and readability over rigid geometric equivalence with simplified contrast and terminals. Designed as an Arabic companion to the Latin Milligram grotesque.
summary: An Arabic companion to Milligram, drawn through the lens of Naskh script to create a bilingual sans-serif system for editorial and complex design work.
description: Designed by Cosimo Lorenzo Pancini with Jordanian type designer Murad Alshawareb, Milligram Arabic approaches Arabic script not by matching Latin shapes but by preserving Naskh rhythm and proportions while simplifying contrast and terminals. Seven weights from Thin to Heavy are available alongside a variable font, covering 207 languages across Latin and Arabic. The family pairs with the 106-style Latin Milligram to form a coherent bilingual system designed as a whole rather than a pairing of separately conceived typefaces.
foundry_blurb: Zetafonts describe Milligram Arabic as a sans-serif exploration through the lens of Naskh scripts, prioritising overall texture and readability over rigid shape-to-shape equivalence with its Latin counterpart. Developed by Cosimo Lorenzo Pancini with the help of Jordanian type designer Murad Alshawareb, it pairs respectfully with the Latin Milligram, creating a bilingual system that feels designed together rather than merely matched.

name: Hagrid Arabic
classification_notes: Hagrid Arabic is a grotesque sans-serif with inverted contrast, brutalist shapes and calligraphic counterspaces; its Arabic component follows a Naskh model reinterpreted through the structural tension of the Latin grotesque.
summary: A bilingual grotesque family that extends Cosimo Lorenzo Pancini's Hagrid into Arabic script via a Naskh model reinterpreted through brutalist proportions and dynamic inktraps.
description: Developed in close collaboration with Murad Alshawareb, the family bridges Latin and Arabic with shared structural logic: brutalist counterspaces and inktraps connect the two scripts, while dense Arabic counters fed back into a new Black weight for the Latin. The family splits into Display styles with tighter metrics and stronger contrast, and Text styles tuned for smoother reading, spanning 13 static styles plus two variable fonts covering weight and optical size axes. Coverage extends to Farsi, Urdu, Extended Latin, Cyrillic and Greek across 216 languages.
foundry_blurb: Zetafonts describe Hagrid Arabic as an expansion of the original Hagrid grotesque, refined in 2025 to improve readability and add Arabic script support. Designed by Cosimo Lorenzo Pancini with Murad Alshawareb, the Arabic component reinterprets a Naskh model through the quirky proportions and structural tension of the Latin design, with the dialogue between scripts described as bidirectional. The family supports Arabic, Farsi and Urdu alongside Extended Latin, Cyrillic and Greek, and includes positional numbers, discretionary ligatures and a playful Monsters stylistic set.

name: Mayson Arabic
classification_notes: A monolinear humanist sans with low contrast, drawing on Arabic calligraphic conventions such as sharp nib rotation angles and fluid curves to inform the Latin letterforms.
summary: A humanist sans by Francesco Canovaro that takes Arabic calligraphic conventions as a starting point for shaping its Latin letterforms.
description: Canovaro used the texture and nib-rotation logic of Arabic script to inform the Latin side of this family, resulting in fluid, open shapes that retain a calligraphic warmth without serif detail. The design is monolinear with low contrast, available in seven weights from Thin to Extrabold plus a variable font, covering 1,102 glyphs across 212 languages in both Arabic and Latin. It sits comfortably in editorial, branding and digital work where a contemporary bilingual voice is required.
foundry_blurb: Zetafonts describe Mayson Arabic as an experiment in using the texture of Arabic script as the foundation for a matching Latin alphabet, with both scripts sharing a reference to flat-nib calligraphy. The monolinear, low-contrast design principle paired with a wide weight range gives the family a bold contemporary look suited to both formal business and creative contexts. The family covers Arabic and Latin in seven weights plus a variable Roman, with support for 212 languages.

name: Monitor
designer: Francesco Canovaro, Cosimo Lorenzo Pancini, Mario De Libero
classification_notes: Monitor is an industrial grotesque with a highly geometric construction, low contrast, and angular stroke joins that deliberately depart from Novarese's hyper-elliptical Eurostile in favour of a sharper, more rational structure.
summary: A 60-style industrial grotesque superfamily spanning five widths, drawing from Eurostile's modular logic but replacing its rounded joints with sharper, more angular geometry.
description: Designed by Francesco Canovaro, Cosimo Lorenzo Pancini and Mario De Libero, Monitor takes the utilitarian structure of Eurostile and Microgramma as its starting point, then strips out their vintage curves in favour of hyper-geometric stroke junctions and accentuated ink traps. The result has a crisper, more assertive character suited to digital interfaces, editorial systems and sports branding. A variable font covering weight, width and italic axes is included in the full family package, alongside OpenType features such as elongated uppercase alternates triggered by triple-repeated capitals.
foundry_blurb: Zetafonts describe Monitor as a contemporary industrial grotesque that reinterprets the mechanical clarity of Eurostile through a sharper, more geometric lens. Where Novarese used hyper-elliptical transitions, Monitor adopts a more rational, angular logic with accentuated ink traps that add visual tension and texture. Spanning 60 styles across five widths with a variable font included in the full package, the family is positioned for responsive digital interfaces, expressive editorial systems and high-impact sports branding, with its design language rooted in 1990s geometric rigidity and rave and club culture aesthetics.

name: Anterior
classification_notes: Anterior explicitly synthesises neo-grotesque structure (Helvetica lineage) with geometric modernist construction (Futura lineage), placing it at the intersection of grotesque and geometric sans-serif traditions.
summary: A 16-style sans-serif by Francesco Canovaro that crosses neo-grotesque rationality with the circular geometry of early Futura, including a stylistic set based on Paul Renner's original geometric drafts.
description: Designed by Francesco Canovaro with contributions from Filippo Sgarlata, Anterior runs from Thin to Extrablack across eight weights with matching italics and a variable font. The family draws structurally on Helvetica's optical discipline and Futura's modular geometry, and a dedicated OpenType stylistic set reproduces Paul Renner's strictest early forms, using circles and rectangles with orthogonal junctions. Coverage extends to 211 languages across Latin script, with small capitals, oldstyle figures, fractions and a broad set of alternates.
foundry_blurb: Zetafonts describe Anterior as a contemporary re-engineering of two 20th-century typographic paradigms: the functional rigour of neo-grotesques and the geometric formalism of early modernist design. Rather than a revival, they position it as a synthesis of rational legibility and geometric expressiveness, with a Renner stylistic set offering strictly geometric forms built exclusively from circles and rectangles for high-impact editorial, branding and signage use.

name: Thicker Narrows
classification_notes: A condensed and compressed extension of the Thicker sports display sans, with squared geometric contours influenced by Eurostile-era space-age aesthetics, intended primarily for high-impact display and advertising use.
summary: A condensed and compressed extension of the Thicker display family, built for sports communication and advertising with squared geometric forms drawn from Eurostile-era aesthetics.
description: Thicker Narrows brings two narrow widths, Condensed and Compressed, to Francesco Canovaro and Andrea Tartarelli's muscular display sans, each spanning ten weights from Thin to Extrablack. The squared contours carry a retro-futurist flavour that nods to Novarese's Eurostile, sitting naturally in sports branding, poster work and logo design. Over 1,024 glyphs cover Latin, Greek and Cyrillic across 218 languages, with OpenType features including small caps, positional numerals and case-sensitive forms.
foundry_blurb: Zetafonts describe Thicker Narrows as a condensed and compressed extension of the original Thicker family, designed by Francesco Canovaro and Andrea Tartarelli and released in 2025. They position the new widths as space-saving but equally muscular, suited to high-impact design, responsive layouts and typographic efficiency, with the same full weight range, 218-language coverage and advanced OpenType feature set as the original.

name: Codec Pro Narrows
designer: Francesco Canovaro, Cosimo Lorenzo Pancini, Andrea Tartarelli, Mario De Libero
classification_notes: Zetafonts describe Codec Pro as strictly geometric in construction, with circular forms and precise proportions throughout. The narrow subfamilies maintain this geometric rigour across three compressed widths.
summary: Three condensed subfamilies extending the Codec Pro geometric sans system across Narrow, Condensed and Compressed widths, each carrying all eleven weights and full Latin, Cyrillic and Greek coverage.
description: A width expansion of Zetafonts' Codec Pro family, adding Narrow, Condensed and Compressed cuts to the original geometric sans system first designed in 2017. All three subfamilies run the full eleven-weight range from Thin to Fat and share the same 1,269-glyph set with support for 218 languages. Stylistic sets allow switching between strict geometric cuts and more open, humanist-influenced shapes, making the family serviceable across branding, editorial, UI and motion design contexts.
foundry_blurb: Zetafonts describe Codec Pro Narrows as more than a width expansion, framing it as a full integration of the Codec system's feature set into three condensed families. They highlight the variety of alternates and stylistic sets that allow designers to shift between a structured modernist aesthetic and softer, humanist-inspired forms, and position the family as a versatile choice for branding, editorial, UI and motion design.

name: Dialogue ME
designer: Cosimo Lorenzo Pancini, Andrea Tartarelli, Oded Ezer, Murad Alshawareb
classification_notes: Rooted in garalde proportions derived from Venetian punchcutters, Dialogue ME merges traditional calligraphic contrast with post-digital brutalist detailing across Latin, Arabic Naskh and Hebrew scripts, with slab and monospaced influences appearing at lighter weights.
summary: A multi-script typeface spanning Latin, Arabic Naskh and Hebrew, reconciling calligraphic tradition with a post-digital brutalist approach across seven weights.
description: Designed by Cosimo Lorenzo Pancini and Andrea Tartarelli with Oded Ezer on Hebrew glyphs and Murad Alshawareb as Arabic consultant, Dialogue ME extends the original Latin Dialogue typeface into a genuinely trilingual family covering 197 languages. Latin forms trace back to Venetian garalde proportions, Arabic follows Naskh calligraphy, and Hebrew draws on mid-20th-century models including Henry Friedländer's Shalom and Aviv. Sharp angles and pixel-perfect counterspaces unify the three scripts, while lighter weights absorb slab and monospaced characteristics that aid readability at small sizes.
foundry_blurb: Zetafonts describe Dialogue ME as a continuation of research begun with the original Latin typeface, pursuing a dialogue between traditional calligraphic letterforms and a post-digital brutalist aesthetic. The family connects pixel-perfect contemporary design with historical proportions: Venetian garalde forms in Latin, Naskh tradition in Arabic, and mid-20th-century Hebrew typography in Hebrew, with the heavier weights taking on pronounced display character while lighter weights blend slab serif and monospaced influences to support text readability.

name: Autarchist
classification_notes: A stylised display family drawing on 1970s–80s sci-fi and horror cover typography, spanning a sans, a flared serif and an exaggerated super serif, making it firmly display-oriented despite its range of weights.
summary: A three-subfamily display family inspired by 1970s and 80s sci-fi and horror cover typography, ranging from a clean sans to an exaggerated super serif with wild, jagged strokes.
description: Designed by Andrea Tartarelli and Cosimo Lorenzo Pancini, Autarchist draws on the cover lettering of pulp paperbacks and genre cinema, with ITC Serif Gothic cited as a starting point for the sans. The family extends across three distinct subfamilies — a sans, a flared serif and a super serif where the serifs grow angular and pronounced — each available in seven weights from Hairline to Extrabold. A variable font spanning both weight and serif intensity axes is included with the full family package, and the release won a Communication Arts Typeface Design award in 2025.
foundry_blurb: Zetafonts describe Autarchist as a typographic odyssey through the aesthetics of 1970s and 80s science fiction and horror, inspired by the cover typography of Asimov, Heinlein, Bradbury and King. The sans began as a reinvention of VGC-era classics like ITC Serif Gothic; Pancini's involvement brought the Super Serif subfamily, where serifs are exaggerated and jagged like the fangs of a creature from a John Carpenter or Dario Argento film. Variable font technology lets the family morph across weight and serif intensity for editorial, packaging and poster use.

name: Arsenica Narrows
classification_notes: Low-contrast Old Style proportions with vintage Italian poster art influences, condensed and narrow cuts that retain humanist serif characteristics drawn from early 20th-century lettering traditions.
summary: A condensed extension of the Arsenica family drawing on early 20th-century Italian poster lettering, offered across six subfamilies with narrow and condensed widths in seven weights.
description: Designed by Francesco Canovaro with contributions from Filippo Sgarlata, the family takes its cues from the poster art of Giorgio Muggiani, whose lettering appeared on campaigns for Cinzano and Pirelli alongside Art Nouveau and Deco visual idioms. Six subfamilies span Narrow and Condensed widths, each available from Thin to Extrabold with italics, alongside upright-only Alt cuts and the Antiqua variant; two variable fonts cover weight and optical size axes. Coverage extends to over 200 languages across Latin, Cyrillic and Greek scripts.
foundry_blurb: Zetafonts describe Arsenica Narrows as the latest extension of the Arsenica superfamily, a condensed version optimised for space-saving use in headlines, posters and editorial layouts. They frame its inspiration as early 20th-century Italian poster art, citing Giorgio Muggiani's integration of Old Style letterforms with Art Nouveau and Deco aesthetics for brands like Cinzano and Pirelli. The family includes extensive OpenType features, over 200 language scripts and two variable fonts, and sits alongside Arsenica Arabic and Arsenica Sans as part of a growing superfamily system.

name: Bogart Arabic
classification_notes: A rounded old-style display design adapted for Arabic script using Maghribi calligraphic conventions, with distinctive ball terminals and chunky, pop-inflected letterforms that sit comfortably in the display category.
summary: An Arabic companion to the Bogart family, drawing on Maghribi calligraphy's rounded forms and ball terminals to carry the original's chunky, playful character into Arabic script.
description: Designed by Cosimo Lorenzo Pancini after Francesco Canovaro's original Bogart, the Arabic extension follows the Maghribi calligraphic tradition, a Moroccan style noted for its unusual rounded shapes and ball terminals. Calligrapher Aziz Bouyabrine helped adapt the traditional model for digital use, balancing playfulness with legibility across nine weights from Thin to Black. A variable font is included with the full family, and coverage spans 217 languages across Latin and Arabic scripts.
foundry_blurb: Zetafonts describe Bogart Arabic as an extension of the Bogart typeface family into Arabic script, inspired by Maghribi calligraphy and its characteristic curves and ball terminals. Designed by Cosimo Lorenzo Pancini with the assistance of calligrapher Aziz Bouyabrine, the typeface was refined for digital usage and is positioned for editorial, packaging and logo design work.

name: Quark
classification_notes: Quark is a serif typeface that combines traditional graceful serifs with geometric treatment of internal letter space, making it a geometric serif rather than a purely classical or transitional style.
summary: A geometric serif family by Mario De Libero that treats the internal space of letterforms as an active design element, particularly in the heavier weights.
description: Designed by Mario De Libero with contributions from Francesco Canovaro, Cosimo Lorenzo Pancini and Andrea Tartarelli, Quark spans seven weights from Extralight to Heavy, each with a matching italic, plus two variable font files. The family's defining characteristic is its bold interpretation of internal counter space, which shifts from conventional emptiness into deliberate geometric form as weights increase. Latin coverage extends to 201 languages across 443 glyphs, with OpenType features including case-sensitive forms and stylistic alternates.
foundry_blurb: Zetafonts describe Quark as elegantly combining serif sophistication with geometric precision, merging typographic tradition with a contemporary edge. They highlight the bold interpretation of internal letter space as the family's core concept, framing it as a typographic universe that extends beyond mere emptiness into a geometric dimension in the bolder weights, turning typography into an exploration of geometry.

name: Heading Deva
