designer: Shrishti Vajpai, Francesco Canovaro, Cosimo Lorenzo Pancini, Andrea Tartarelli
classification_notes: An industrial grotesque extended to Devanagari script, sharing the same skeletal structure and weight-width system as the Latin Heading superfamily. Classified as grotesque due to its bold, industrial character and absence of calligraphic influence.
summary: A Devanagari extension of the Heading superfamily, bringing its industrial grotesque structure across eight weights and eight widths to Hindi and other Devanagari-script languages.
description: Designed by Shrishti Vajpai as an expansion of the Heading superfamily originally created by Francesco Canovaro, Cosimo Lorenzo Pancini and Andrea Tartarelli, Heading Deva applies the same industrial grotesque skeleton to Devanagari. The family spans 64 static styles across eight weights from Thin to Heavy and eight widths from Ultracompressed to Wide, plus a variable font covering both axes. Script coverage extends to Latin, Cyrillic and Greek alongside Devanagari, supporting over 220 languages.
foundry_blurb: Zetafonts describe Heading Deva as a powerful tool for headlines, branding, editorial and digital design, giving Devanagari-script users the same range of weights and widths as the Latin original. Designed by Shrishti Vajpai in 2023 with the original Heading team, it bridges the gap between Latin and Devanagari by extending the bold industrial grotesque skeleton of the Heading superfamily to one of the world's most widely used writing systems.

name: Claus
classification_notes: Claus is rooted in the Old Style letterforms of the hand-lettered Stunt Roman, redrawn with etched serifs, calligraphic quirks and narrow Art Deco proportions, placing it firmly in the Old Style serif tradition with display characteristics.
summary: A reinvention of the 1929 Stunt Roman hand-lettered alphabet, redrawn across eight weights with matching italics and calligraphic details borrowed from Art Deco penmanship.
description: Mario De Libero drew Claus from Ross Frederick George's Stunt Roman, first published in the Speedball Text Book in 1929, adding etched serifs and calligraphic alternates to bring the narrow Old Style forms into contemporary use. The family runs from Thin to Black with a full italic companion added in 2024, plus two variable fonts spanning the weight axis. OpenType features include small caps, discretionary ligatures and stylistic sets, making it suited to editorial layouts, luxury packaging and festive branding.
foundry_blurb: Zetafonts describe Claus as a festive reinvention of the classic Stunt Roman, originally crafted by penmanship virtuoso Ross Frederick George and first published in the Speedball Text Book in 1929. Designer Mario De Libero expanded the original single-weight into a full eight-weight family with a brutalist twist, introducing etched serifs, calligraphic quirks and festive decorative elements, before adding a complete italic range to bring expressive, whimsical energy to the project.

name: Mayson
classification_notes: Monolinear, low-contrast strokes with open humanist shapes drawn from both Latin calligraphic tradition and Arabic flat-nib calligraphy, placing it firmly in the humanist sans-serif category.
summary: A humanist sans by Francesco Canovaro that takes Arabic flat-nib calligraphy as a structural reference for its Latin letterforms, resulting in sharp nib angles and fluid, open shapes across seven weights.
description: Designed as an experiment in cross-script calligraphic influence, Mayson adapts elements of Arabic script tradition, particularly sharp nib-rotation angles and fluid stroke rhythm, into a monolinear Latin design. The family spans seven weights from Thin to Extrabold, each with a matching italic, plus two variable font axes and a companion Arabic subfamily. Its 916 glyphs cover Latin, Greek and Cyrillic across 214 languages, with OpenType features including oldstyle figures, stylistic sets and fractions.
foundry_blurb: Zetafonts describe Mayson as a humanist sans designed by Francesco Canovaro, framed as an experiment in using Arabic script texture as the basis for a Latin alphabet. They highlight the shared reference to flat-nib calligraphy as the unifying thread between the two scripts, and note that the monolinear, low-contrast design principle gives the family a contemporary look suited to both formal and creative applications.

name: Mezura
classification_notes: Mezura draws on the circular proportions of Naskh calligraphy and Old Style Latin forms, blending geometric structure with calligraphic movement across both Arabic and Latin scripts. The mono-width approach and display-oriented extremes place it within a geometric humanist tradition.
summary: A bilingual Arabic and Latin typeface by Cosimo Lorenzo Pancini that uses a mono-width approach derived from Naskh calligraphy and Old Style proportions, keeping character widths constant across seven weights.
description: Unusually, the design began with Arabic Naskh rather than Latin, with the Latin letters subsequently drawn to echo their Arabic counterparts. The mono-width constraint means each letter occupies the same horizontal space regardless of weight, producing a distinctive compressed density in the heavier cuts that mirrors how Arabic letterforms thicken in manuscript traditions. Light and Fat weights are tuned for display use while the Regular maximises on-screen and print readability with open counters.
foundry_blurb: Zetafonts describe Mezura as a typographic exploration of the relationship between geometry and the art of writing, drawing on the discovery that both Old Style Latin letterforms and Arabic Naskh calligraphy share an underlying framework of proportions anchored by the circle. Designed by Cosimo Lorenzo Pancini and released in 2023, the family spans seven styles plus a variable font, covers 177 languages across Arabic and Latin scripts, and features OpenType details including standard ligatures, discretionary ligatures and variant Arabic letter forms.

name: Aquawax FX
classification_notes: Aquawax FX is built on a geometric sans foundation derived from the original Aquawax, distinguished by counter-inktraps and softened contours that give it a fluid, watery character while retaining geometric construction.
summary: A reworking of Zetafonts' own Aquawax that swaps inktraps for counter-inktraps, softening corners to produce a distinctly liquid, flowing appearance suited to display and identity work.
description: Designed by Francesco Canovaro and released in 2022, Aquawax FX departs from the 2008 original by introducing counter-inktraps — rounded inner and outer corners that create a watery visual quality while preserving the original terminal cuts. The family spans nine weights from Thin to Heavy with matching italics across two sub-families: the standard Roman and the stencil-influenced Space variant aimed at logos and science-fiction headings. Variable font versions are included with the full family package, and coverage extends to Latin, Greek and Cyrillic across 218 languages.
foundry_blurb: Zetafonts describe Aquawax FX as a contemporary evolution of the Aquawax family, developed by Francesco Canovaro to revisit the original 2008 design with new geometric inventions and a fluid zeitgeist. The counter-inktraps create a distinctive watery aesthetic by rounding inner and outer corners while retaining original terminal cuts, and the Space variant extends this experimentation with a stencil-like style suited to logos and sci-fi headings.

name: Gronau
classification_notes: The family is explicitly blackletter-inspired, rooted in a 1902 geometric gothic typeface. Each variant reinterprets gothic letterforms through different lenses: digital revival, inline 3D display, and contemporary calligraphic condensed.
summary: A three-part blackletter family by Andrea Tartarelli, built from a digital revival of Wilhelm Gronau's 1902 Fette Reichs-Deutsch and extended into inline and calligraphic display variants.
description: Gronau Fette begins as a faithful digitisation of the original's broad, square gothic structure, characterised by mild contrast, geometric shapes and clean 45-degree angles. Gronau Inline transforms those same nib-cut strokes into a 3D ribbon effect that gives the gothic forms a sporty, dynamic energy. Gronau Neue departs most from the source, using condensed proportions and a generous x-height to arrive at a calligraphic contemporary blackletter suited to editorial and packaging contexts.
foundry_blurb: Zetafonts describe Gronau as rooted in a 1912 Berlin specimen for Fette Reichs-Deutsch from Wilhelm Gronaus Schriftgießerei. Designer Andrea Tartarelli digitised the original as Gronau Fette, then pushed its pre-modernist geometric gothic logic toward display use with an inline ribbon variant and a condensed calligraphic interpretation called Gronau Neue. All styles include a wide range of OpenType features and glyph alternates covering 196 languages.

name: Freitag
classification_notes: A heavy sans serif with humanist condensed proportions, flared stems and reverse contrast, drawing on 1970s photo-lettering and display typography traditions rather than text use.
summary: A 1970s-inflected display sans by Cosimo Lorenzo Pancini, built on reverse contrast and flared stems that escalate into full bell-bottom exuberance at heavier weights.
description: Freitag draws on the buoyant photo-lettering and rub-on transfer typography of the 1970s, referencing designers such as Benguiat, Novarese, Motter and Excoffon. The Roman subfamily progresses from a controlled medium-contrast Light through to a Heavy where reverse contrast becomes unmistakable, while the Display subfamily pushes further still with swash capitals, connected italics and optical sizing marked as M, L and XL. The full family ships as 16 static styles plus four variable fonts covering weight and contrast axes, with 587 glyphs supporting 205 languages across extended Latin.
foundry_blurb: Zetafonts describe Freitag as Cosimo Lorenzo Pancini's homage to the vivacious typography of the 1970s, when psychedelia, Pop culture and low-cost photo-lettering technologies pushed designers towards bubbly, high-spirited lettering. The main family builds design exuberance along the weight axis like anticipation for the weekend, while the Display subfamily introduces stronger calligraphic letterforms and connected italics for logo design and ultra-expressive editorial work.

name: Stadio Now Deva
classification_notes: The typeface carries a reverse contrast skeleton derived from Aldo Novarese's original Stadio design, adapted for Devanagari script. Its strong horizontal-to-vertical contrast inversion and headline-oriented construction firmly place it in the display category.
summary: A Devanagari extension of Aldo Novarese's reverse contrast Stadio design, adapted by Shrishti Vajpai to bridge Latin and Indic scripts for display use.
description: Shrishti Vajpai extended the quirky reverse contrast skeleton of Stadio, originally designed by Novarese for rub transfers and digitised in 2020, into Devanagari across six weights from Extralight to Extrabold with a variable font axis. The adaptation follows the Latin revival work by Cosimo Lorenzo Pancini and Andrea Tartarelli, aiming to carry Novarese's vernacular energy into Hindic scripts. It suits headline typography, branding, editorial and packaging where attention-grabbing display setting in Devanagari is needed.
foundry_blurb: Zetafonts describe Stadio Now Deva as bringing Aldo Novarese's original vision for Stadio into the realm of Devanagari script. Adapted by Shrishti Vajpai to honour the legacy of the Italian type design master and introduce his work to a new audience, the face extends the reverse contrast Latin skeleton to Hindic scripts, offering space-saving expressive typography for headlines, branding, editorial design and packaging across 199 languages.

name: Targa Pro
designer: Cosimo Lorenzo Pancini, Francesco Canovaro, Mario de Libero
classification_notes: A geometric, condensed sans-serif with slightly rounded corners derived from Italian licence plate lettering; the family includes both monospaced and proportional subfamilies as well as stencil and handmade variants.
summary: A geometric sans originally drawn from Italian licence plate lettering, rebuilt twenty years on into a full multi-weight family with both monospaced and proportional subfamilies.
description: First released in 2003 as Zetafonts' debut typeface, Targa Pro was substantially redesigned by Francesco Canovaro with the original's condensed, slightly rounded geometry preserved but resolved into a more consistent five-weight system. The family splits into Targa Pro Mono and Targa Pro Roman, joined by a stencil and a handdrawn variant for display use. Variable font support is included in the full family package, and the character set covers 213 languages across 673 glyphs.
foundry_blurb: Zetafonts describe Targa Pro as inspired by the peculiar monospaced sans-serif letterforms found on Italian car licence plates for many years. Originally designed by Cosimo Lorenzo Pancini in 2003, the family was comprehensively redesigned by Francesco Canovaro, retaining its inventions whilst addressing its shortcomings. The result spans five weights plus italics in both monospaced and proportional widths, alongside Targa Pro Stencil and Targa Pro Hand, with extended Latin coverage for over 200 languages and a full suite of OpenType features.

name: Salad
classification_notes: A condensed display sans rooted in hand-painted signage aesthetics, with decorative interlocking and inline variants that place it firmly in the display category rather than workhorse text typography.
summary: A condensed display sans inspired by hand-painted ballroom lettering found in Fuerteventura, spanning five weights with roman, italic, interlocking and inline variants.
description: Francesco Canovaro reworked Debora Manetti's earlier typeface Sala de Fiestas into a fuller family, preserving the original's condensed proportions and vernacular Latino character while extending it with a five-weight range, Cyrillic support for 213 languages, and a broad set of OpenType features including small caps, stylistic alternates and old-style figures. The Interlock subfamily adds interlocking letterform variants for tighter display settings, and the Inline weight extends the decorative possibilities further. Hand-drawn dingbats complement the signpainting feel throughout.
foundry_blurb: Zetafonts describe Salad as a grand tour into signpainter aesthetics, born from hand-painted letterforms Debora Manetti discovered on the walls of a ballroom on the island of Fuerteventura. Francesco Canovaro built the family from that starting point, delivering extreme versatility through five weights, extended character sets and OpenType features, alongside interlocking and inline variants suited to display and packaging work.

name: Coco Tardis
designer: Cosimo Lorenzo Pancini, Mario De Libero
classification_notes: Coco Tardis derives from Coco Gothic, a geometric sans-serif, and varies its letterforms across historical eras using a variable font axis, maintaining the geometric sans structure throughout.
summary: A variable sans-serif that morphs its letterforms across a timeline from 1870 to 2022, encoding typographic period styles into a single font slider.
description: Built as an extension of Coco Gothic Pro, Coco Tardis uses a variable font axis labelled 'VNTG' to shift the letterforms through period-appropriate alternates decade by decade. The family ships in 27 static styles across nine eras and three weights, plus a variable file that lets the user scrub through the full timeline. Coverage runs to 213 languages across Latin script with 814 glyphs per style.
foundry_blurb: Zetafonts describe Coco Tardis as the first time-travelling typeface, born from the historical alternates built into Coco Gothic Pro. Rather than switching between style sets manually, a variable font slider lets users select a date and the typeface takes on a period-relevant appearance, making it easier to use than a TARDIS or a DeLorean.

name: Swanstone
classification_notes: Described explicitly as a display typeface, Swanstone draws on early 20th-century Italian serif traditions, particularly Nebiolo's Raffaello and ITC Benguiat, blending high contrast classical proportions with an edgy, experimental character suited to display use.
summary: A high-contrast display serif rooted in early 20th-century Italian typography, particularly the Nebiolo foundry's Raffaello, with an edgy tension between classical elegance and contemporary sharpness.
description: Designed by Mario De Libero and released in 2021, Swanstone draws on historic Italian type, especially the Nebiolo foundry's Raffaello, and channels the dramatic weight contrast of ITC Benguiat into a more personal, experimental form. The family spans six weights from Extralight to Extrabold, each with a matching italic, plus variable Roman and italic axes, and covers Latin and Cyrillic across 216 languages. Signature details in the lowercase 'a' and 's' give it a recognisable character suited to fashion editorial, poster work and high-end branding.
foundry_blurb: Zetafonts describe Swanstone as a display typeface reimagining early 20th-century serif traditions through a contemporary lens. Born from Mario De Libero's study of historic Italian typography, particularly Nebiolo's Raffaello, it blends classical proportions and contrast with an experimental, personal aesthetic influenced by ITC Benguiat. Originally a single weight in Zetafonts' Singularity line, it has since expanded to 12 styles plus variable fonts, with Cyrillic support, making it suited to editorial design, posters, branding and the fashion industry.

name: Visconte
classification_notes: Described explicitly as a 'brutal serif', Visconte builds on an old-style calligraphic structure but pushes contrast and distortion to extremes, making it unsuitable for text use and firmly a display typeface.
summary: A self-described 'brutal serif' by Andrea Tartarelli, built on an old-style calligraphic skeleton and distorted into spiky, high-contrast forms inspired by Italo Calvino's The Cloven Viscount.
description: Designed as the signature typeface for the Desina Graphic Design Festival 2023, Visconte extends Zetafonts' Calvino family by pushing the contrast axis far beyond the Grande subfamily, introducing deliberate distortions and unexpected stroke connections. The family comprises two subfamilies, Visconte and Visconte Reale, each available in four weights from Regular to Black, plus a variable font. Its raw, spiky serifs and medievalised texture suit editorial headlines, posters and book covers where high tension between elegance and rupture is the point.
foundry_blurb: Zetafonts describe Visconte as an evocative addition to the Calvino family, created in collaboration with Cosimo Lorenzo Pancini and Francesco Canovaro, and conceptually rooted in Italo Calvino's Il Visconte Dimezzato. The typeface embodies the duality of a nobleman split in two, with the distinctive lowercase 'i' referencing that theme of division. It is intended for designers who want to inject a rugged, enigmatic medieval spirit into headlines, covers and poster work.

name: Calvino Arabic
classification_notes: A multi-script humanist sans spanning Arabic and Latin, designed with calligraphic fluidity balanced against modern clarity; the Arabic forms honour traditional calligraphy while aligning with the contemporary proportions of the Latin Calvino family.
summary: The Arabic counterpart to Zetafonts' Calvino family, blending traditional Arabic calligraphic rhythm with a clean, modern editorial voice across four weights and matching italics.
description: Designed by Andrea Tartarelli and released in 2024, Calvino Arabic draws on Italo Calvino's Six Memos for the Next Millennium as a conceptual framework, aiming for clarity and fluidity in equal measure. The family runs to eight styles from Regular to Extrabold with italic companions, supported by two variable fonts covering weight axes in both roman and italic. Coverage spans Arabic, Latin and Cyrillic across 205 languages and 2031 glyphs, with OpenType features including old-style figures, fractions and stylistic sets.
foundry_blurb: Zetafonts describe Calvino Arabic as the Arabic extension of their Calvino typeface, built around the principles Italo Calvino outlined in Six Memos for the Next Millennium. Still in beta, the design balances clarity and fluidity while respecting traditional Arabic calligraphy. The current release covers essential weights, with plans to expand the range and add further refinements to serve a wide variety of editorial applications.

name: Stadio Now
designer: Aldo Novarese (revival by Cosimo Lorenzo Pancini & Andrea Tartarelli; Cyrillic by Vika Usmanova)
classification_notes: Stadio Now is classified as a display sans owing to its defining reverse contrast, where horizontal strokes are thicker than verticals, a style Novarese himself described as a 19th-century 'Italian' style. The family extends into text weights but its character is fundamentally display-oriented.
summary: A 2021 revival of Aldo Novarese's 1974 reverse-contrast sans, expanded by Zetafonts into a 26-style variable family covering text and display sizes.
description: Originally designed for the dry transfer brand R41 in 1974, Stadio employs the 'Italian' style in which horizontal strokes outweigh verticals, creating letters that are simultaneously sturdy and unsettling. Zetafonts rebuilt the design from specimens in Novarese's book Il Segno Alfabetico, extending it across twelve weight grades from Sottile to Weirdo, each with an italic, plus two variable fonts with weight and optical size axes. The revival supports Latin and Cyrillic scripts across 200 languages and won the PRINT Award for Typeface Design in 2021.
foundry_blurb: Zetafonts describe Stadio Now as a revival of an Aldo Novarese design published by the dry transfer brand R41 in 1974, notable for its reverse contrast and enlarged horizontal proportions, which Novarese himself called a decorative display type in the 19th-century Italian style but sans serif. Released in 2021 to mark Novarese's centenary, the expanded family features text and display variants, a variable font exploring the reverse-contrast design space, and Cyrillic support added by Vika Usmanova.

name: Domotika Pro
classification_notes: Domotika combines open humanist letterforms with slight calligraphic curve endings, placing it firmly in the humanist sans tradition. Its semi-condensed proportions and architectural pragmatism distinguish it from geometric or grotesque models.
summary: A humanist sans with calligraphic curve endings and semi-condensed proportions, designed for editorial and display work across Latin, Greek and Cyrillic scripts.
description: Originally designed by Cosimo Lorenzo Pancini in 2018, the family was substantially expanded in 2021 by Andrea Tartarelli, who reworked the italics and added over 500 glyphs to extend coverage to 219 languages across three scripts. Open humanist letterforms are tempered by slight calligraphic curve endings, lending the typeface personality without sacrificing legibility. Sixteen styles from Thin to Black with matching italics, plus a broad OpenType feature set including small caps, oldstyle figures and stylistic sets, make it a practical choice for text and display settings alike.
foundry_blurb: Zetafonts describe Domotika as an attempt to translate modernist and humanist ideals into typographic form, drawing inspiration from architectural practice in the spirit of Mies van der Rohe and Flaubert's attention to detail. The name references 'domotics', robotic home technology, and the typeface is positioned as a workhorse family with a humanist soul and semi-condensed feel suited to editorial and display use where readability, personality and space efficiency must coexist.

name: Sunshine Pro
designer: Francesco Canovaro, Cosimo Lorenzo Pancini, Solenn Bordeau
classification_notes: Described explicitly as a Clarendon-style font with variable contrast across the weight axis, placing it firmly in the slab serif tradition with its bracketed serifs and modulated stroke contrast.
summary: A Clarendon-style slab with an unusual variable contrast axis, where stroke contrast inverts at light weights and intensifies at heavy weights.
description: Originating as part of Zetafonts' Quarantype collection, designed as a response to the Covid-19 pandemic, this typeface extends Francesco Canovaro's original Sunshine design. Its defining characteristic is a contrast axis that reverses in the Light weight, flattens near Regular and then climbs sharply through Bold and Heavy. A variable font covers the full weight range, allowing precise positioning across the design space. Available free for personal and commercial use across 108 languages in Latin and Greek scripts.
foundry_blurb: Zetafonts describe Sunshine Pro as an experimental Clarendon-style font developed by Cosimo Lorenzo Pancini and Solenn Bordeau, expanding on Francesco Canovaro's original Sunshine design from the Quarantype collection. The family features reversed contrast at light weights, minimal contrast at regular, and spiking contrast in the heavier cuts, with a variable version enabling full exploration of the design space. It is offered free for personal and commercial use as part of Zetafonts' likefunding initiative on Instagram.

name: Etrusco Now
designer: Cosimo Lorenzo Pancini, Mario De Libero
classification_notes: A revival of the early 1920s Nebiolo grotesque, itself derived from Schelter & Giesecke's Grotesk; sits firmly in the grotesque sans-serif tradition with a slightly vintage character distinguishing it from cold late-modernist grotesques.
summary: A revival of the early 1920s Nebiolo grotesque expanded into a superfamily of 42 styles across three widths and seven weights, with italics and variable font support.
description: Originally cast in lead by Italian foundry Nebiolo and inspired by Schelter & Giesecke's Grotesk, the revival extends the source material into 42 styles spanning Compressed, Condensed and Roman widths. Lighter normal-width weights draw on the modernist character of Recta, while the heavy compressed and condensed cuts reference Aldo Novarese's Metropol, lending the family a warmth absent from Helvetica-era grotesques. The Etrusco Classic subfamily preserves the original historical weights alongside the expanded contemporary range.
foundry_blurb: Zetafonts describe Etrusco Now as a revival of a lead typeface cast by Italian foundry Nebiolo in the early 1920s, itself heavily inspired by Schelter & Giesecke's Grotesk. The Italiantype team directed by Cosimo Lorenzo Pancini and Mario de Libero extended the original weight and width range to create a 21-style superfamily in three widths with matching italics, infusing the design with references to Nebiolo classics Recta and Metropol. They position it as contemporary but rich in historical quirks, suited to editorial and branding work where subtle personality is preferred over formal neutrality.

name: Erotique Sans
designer: Cosimo Lorenzo Pancini, Maria Chiara Fantini, Solenn Bordeau
classification_notes: A serif-free, high-contrast sans with Didone-influenced stroke modulation, designed explicitly for display and titling use rather than text setting. Its dramatic thick-thin contrast and romantic curves place it firmly in the display category.
summary: A high-contrast sans with Didone-like stroke modulation, designed for display and titling work where restrained elegance and an edgy contemporary character are needed.
description: Designed by Cosimo Lorenzo Pancini and Maria Chiara Fantini, Erotique Sans strips the serifs from the Erotique family whilst retaining its didonesque contrast and assertively feminine character. The family spans four weights with matching Alternate cuts providing access to exotic letterforms, a Flourishes set of whiplike fleurons for logo and titling work, and a dedicated Text Regular with reduced contrast for longer-form use. Over 565 glyphs cover 190 languages in extended Latin, with OpenType support for stylistic alternates, ligatures, swash forms and multiple numeral styles.
foundry_blurb: Zetafonts describe Erotique Sans as the sans serif counterpart to Erotique, itself an evolution of the Lovelace design that blends romantic curves with a glitchy, fluid neo-brutalist aesthetic. They position it as a display typeface with didonesque elegance, pairing high-contrast weights and an Alternate family for exotic letterforms with a Flourishes set for interlocking ornamental use and a Text weight for editorial applications at smaller sizes.

name: Erotique
designer: Cosimo Lorenzo Pancini, Maria Chiara Fantini, Solenn Bordeau, Andrea Tartarelli
classification_notes: A high-contrast oldstyle-influenced serif designed expressly for display use, with calligraphic distortions and quirky alternate letterforms that push it firmly into the display category rather than conventional text serif.
summary: A high-contrast display serif from Zetafonts that fuses oldstyle letterforms with calligraphic distortions and an extensive set of alternate glyphs for editorial and branding work.
description: Designed by Cosimo Lorenzo Pancini and Maria Chiara Fantini, Erotique builds on Zetafonts' earlier Lovelace, pushing its romantic curves toward something sharper and more unsettling. Four weights from Monoline to Bold are available in both standard and Alternate cuts, with a companion sans, a text variant and two ornament packs rounding out the family. Over 500 glyphs per style cover 190 languages in extended Latin, with OpenType features including stylistic alternates across nine sets, swash forms and discretionary ligatures.
foundry_blurb: Zetafonts describe Erotique as an evolution of Lovelace that challenges romantic curves with a glitchy, fluid aesthetic of transmodern neo-brutalist typography. They frame its oldstyle Pfeiffer-like letter shapes as an 'evil serif' made edgier by quirky connections and unexpected calligraphic twirls that marry digital distortion to traditional penmanship. The family won Paris Design Awards in 2021 in both Typeface Design and Communication Design categories.

name: Casagrande
designer: Alberto Casagrande, Manuel Alvaro, Valentino Coppi, Mario De Libero
classification_notes: A multi-family display collection spanning Art Deco, modernist, geometric and script styles, all rooted in Italian poster and product design of the 1930s and 40s. The diversity of sub-families makes display the only appropriate top-level category.
summary: A 24-font display collection drawing on Italian poster and product design of the 1930s and 40s, built around six stylistically distinct sub-families.
description: Developed in 2020 by the Italiantype Team in collaboration with Italian lettering artist and illustrator Alberto Casagrande, the collection bundles six display families into a single package. Sub-families range from the Art Deco-influenced Antifascista and Deco (each with dithering or inline variants) to the modernist Casabau, the geometric Grind in four widths, and the script families Reclame and Casatiello. The package also includes an ornaments icon font, making it a self-contained toolkit for poster work, book covers and vintage-themed illustration.
foundry_blurb: Zetafonts describe Casagrande as a suite of closely related typefaces inspired by Alberto Casagrande's colourful, vintage-themed illustration style, intended to replicate the nostalgic charm of Italian poster and product design from the thirties and forties. They cite two-colour overprints, coarse dithering, handmade calligraphy, Art Deco reminiscences, modernist hints and pop culture references as ingredients, and pitch the collection at illustrators, poster artists and book cover designers.

name: Garbata
classification_notes: Garbata takes its skeleton from old-style serif models like Windsor and Cooper, applying a flat-brush calligraphic sensibility to produce a sans with distinctly humanist, quirky letterforms rather than the neutral geometry of grotesque or modernist traditions.
summary: A sans serif by Francesco Canovaro that borrows its skeleton from old-style faces like Windsor and Cooper, adding a flat-brush calligraphic quality across eight weights with matching italics.
description: Rather than following grotesque or modernist models, Garbata draws on the quirky, sloped forms of old-style typefaces and flat-brush calligraphy, producing a sans that reads warmly at text sizes and shows character at display scale. The family runs from Thin to Black with matching italics, 861 glyphs, and coverage of over 200 languages across Latin and Cyrillic scripts. A variable font is included with the full family package, and the face ships with a thorough OpenType feature set including small caps, oldstyle figures and multiple stylistic sets.
foundry_blurb: Zetafonts describe Garbata as a sans designed by Francesco Canovaro with Andrea Tartarelli and Sofia Bandini in 2020, conceived as a departure from over-exploited grotesque and modernist models. It takes its skeleton from old-style faces like Windsor and Cooper, blending their sloped, quirky letterforms with a flat-brush calligraphic sensibility. The result is a humble yet distinctive typeface that is readable in body text and shows elegant, whimsical qualities in logo and display use, with finely-tuned differences between text-oriented and display-oriented weights.

name: Cairoli Now
designer: Cosimo Lorenzo Pancini, Mario De Libero
classification_notes: A revival of the 1928 Nebiolo grotesque Cairoli, itself a license of Wagner & Schmidt's Neue moderne Grotesk, placing it firmly in the nineteenth-century grotesque tradition alongside Akzidenz-Grotesk and Aurora.
summary: A 2020 revival of the 1928 Nebiolo grotesque Cairoli, expanded into a superfamily with a contemporary Now branch and a faithful Classic branch, each available across three widths and seven weights.
description: Originally cast by Italian foundry Nebiolo as a license of Wagner & Schmidt's Neue moderne Grotesk, Cairoli predated the grotesque superfamily format by decades. The Now branch raises the x-height for text and digital use, adds inktraps and round punctuation, and slightly softens the curves, while the Classic branch preserves the original low x-height and display character, including hook cuts in curved letters, the high-waisted R and squared ovals. The full family runs to 84 static styles plus four variable fonts covering weight and width axes, with OpenType features and coverage of over 200 Latin-script languages.
foundry_blurb: Zetafonts describe Cairoli Now as a revival directed by Cosimo Lorenzo Pancini and Mario De Libero, originally rediscovered at a TypeThursday Florence meeting in 2019. They developed both a Classic version that normalises and emphasises the original design's peculiarities, and a Now variant with a higher x-height and deeper inktraps suited for contemporary text use, framing the project as an exercise in subtlety and affection for lost letterforms.

name: Noctis
designer: Luca Terzo, Cosimo Lorenzo Pancini, Valentino Coppi
classification_notes: Strong vertical contrast and triangular wedge serifs place Noctis firmly in the Didone tradition, drawing direct inspiration from Aldo Novarese's Primate (1972) while adding sharp, fashion-inflected detailing.
summary: A high-contrast didone with sharp triangular serifs, drawn from Novarese's Primate and pitched at fashion editorial and identity work.
description: Designed by Luca Terzo and developed by the Zetafonts team, Noctis runs across five weights from Thin to Heavy, each with a true italic, totalling ten upright and italic styles. The family extends into a Texturae subfamily where individual letterforms tile into seamless decorative patterns for branding use. Didonesque contrast and distinctive wedge serifs give it presence at display sizes without entirely sacrificing text legibility, and 481 glyphs cover 197 Latin-script languages.
foundry_blurb: Zetafonts describe Noctis as originating from a single-weight display typeface inspired by Novarese's 1972 Primate for Berthold, later expanded into a full family of five weights with true italics and a decorative Texturae subfamily. They position it within the aesthetics of Italian fashion of the 1980s and 90s, combining didonesque contrast with triangular serifs for use in logo design, editorial work and branding applications.

name: Stinger
designer: Cosimo Lorenzo Pancini, Maria Chiara Fantini, Andrea Tartarelli
classification_notes: Stinger uses reverse contrast throughout, subverting the conventional thick-vertical/thin-horizontal relationship. The design draws on the tradition of 19th-century Italian reverse-contrast types while applying the systematic width and weight grid of modernist grotesques like Univers.
summary: A reverse-contrast super-family of 40 styles across four widths and five weights, rooted in 19th-century Italian type specimens and cross-bred with the systematic rigour of modernist grotesques.
description: Stinger takes the reverse-contrast tradition that dates to William Caslon's 1821 specimens and applies it to a Univers-style grid of four widths (Slim, Normal, Fit and Wide) plus a Text subfamily, each running from Thin to Heavy with true italics. The result is 40 fonts covering 218 languages across Latin extended, Cyrillic and Arabic, with two variable fonts providing continuous control over both weight and width axes. Calligraphic terminals in the lighter weights give way to sculptural, logo-ready forms in the heaviest cuts, keeping personality consistent across the range.
foundry_blurb: Zetafonts describe Stinger as a marriage of the subversive reverse-contrast tradition with the workhorse approach of modernist typefaces like Univers. Designed by Cosimo Lorenzo Pancini, Maria Chiara Fantini, Andrea Tartarelli and Francesco Canovaro, the family was built to offer full typesetting range: Normal and Fit for text, Wide for display and titling, and Slim for space-constrained settings. A variable version is included with the full family package.

name: Quarantype
designer: Cosimo Lorenzo Pancini, Maria Chiara Fantini, Andrea Tartarelli, Francesco Canovaro, Debora Manetti
classification_notes: A collection of ten stylistically distinct display typefaces spanning playful, calligraphic and experimental styles, born from the #36daysoftype challenge; no single structural classification applies across all ten.
summary: A collection of ten free display typefaces made by the Zetafonts team during Italy's March 2020 lockdown, each style drawn from their #36daysoftype challenge entries.
description: Ten distinct display faces developed in quick succession as the Zetafonts studio worked through the #36daysoftype challenge during the first Covid lockdown. The styles range from playful and calligraphic to experimental, each reflecting the team's ongoing research into contemporary trends and historical lettering. Released free for commercial and non-commercial use, the collection covers 186 languages across a Latin character set of 359 glyphs.
foundry_blurb: Zetafonts describe Quarantype as a community gift born from their isolation during the Coronavirus outbreak of March 2020, selecting the strongest glyphs from their daily type challenge to build ten free typefaces. Though made quickly, each face draws on the studio's research into contemporary trends and historical design, bridging calligraphy and type design, and all styles are free to use commercially and non-commercially without restriction.

name: Aristotelica Pro
designer: Cosimo Lorenzo Pancini, Francesco Canovaro, Andrea Tartarelli
classification_notes: A rounded geometric sans built on circular forms, with strict geometric construction softened by consistent rounding across all strokes, placing it firmly in the geometric sans tradition.
summary: A large-family rounded geometric sans by Zetafonts, covering display and text optical sizes, condensed cuts, an icons set and variable fonts across nine weights.
description: Built on the philosophy of circular simplicity and named after Aristotle, the typeface spans 43 static styles plus two variable fonts across display, text and condensed subfamilies. A companion icons subfamily uses matching monoline weights to pair seamlessly with the text. Language coverage extends to over 200 languages across Latin, Greek, Cyrillic and Arabic scripts, with over 1,100 glyphs and OpenType features including small caps, old-style figures and stylistic alternates.
foundry_blurb: Zetafonts describe Aristotelica Pro as a 2020 redesign of their classic rounded geometric sans, originally conceived as an exercise in restraint and simplicity and named after the philosopher who pioneered formal logic. The Pro version expands the original family with condensed cuts for both display and text, adds Arabic script support bringing total coverage to over 218 languages, and includes Aristotelica Icons, a set of variable-width monoline icons designed to match the typeface's stroke weights.

name: Boring Sans
classification_notes: A sans-serif family spanning neutral workhorse proportions through to exaggerated brutalist letterforms, with variable axes for weight and weirdness placing it firmly in the grotesque tradition but with expressive extensions.
summary: A variable sans-serif by Cosimo Lorenzo Pancini built across three subfamilies that range from a neutral text workhorse to a high-contrast, inktrap-heavy brutalist extreme.
description: Designed by Cosimo Lorenzo Pancini and released in 2020, the family uses two variable axes, weight and weirdness, to map a continuous range from the restrained proportions of the A subfamily through the slanted strokes and deep inktraps of B to the deliberately bombastic letterforms of C. Each subfamily runs across five weights with matching italics, totalling 30 styles, with two variable font files available in the full package. The extended Latin glyph set covers 204 languages, and several stylistic sets offer alternate forms for further customisation.
foundry_blurb: Zetafonts describe Boring Sans as a family designed along two variable axes, weight and weirdness, allowing exploration from a neutral, readable sans through to strongly contrasted and dynamic letterforms intended to raise eyebrows on social media. The A subfamily is a traditional workhorse, B takes a contemporary brutal approach with slanted lines and deep inktraps, and C pushes every feature to a bombastic extreme. The full family includes variable font versions for precise control.

name: Thicker
classification_notes: A geometric sans-serif with squared, slightly rounded terminals drawing on Eurostile's proportions, built for high-impact display use in sports and advertising contexts.
summary: A geometric sans spanning ten weights from Thin to Extrablack, built around the muscular proportions of sports typography with a nod to Novarese's Eurostile.
description: Designed by Francesco Canovaro and Andrea Tartarelli, Thicker began at the extrablack end of the weight range before being extended downward into lighter cuts where vintage-inspired proportions and slightly squared forms emerge. Three alternate italic forms with variable slant add the dynamism expected of sports and advertising work. The family covers 44 styles including Inline and Destroy variants, with two variable fonts spanning the full weight axis, and a character set of over 900 glyphs across Latin, Greek and Cyrillic covering 218 languages.
foundry_blurb: Zetafonts describe Thicker as a geometric sans typeface on steroids, first drawn in the extrablack weight with the aesthetics of high-power dynamic sports communication, then developed into lighter weights that reveal vintage-inspired proportions and a squared look nodding to Novarese's Eurostile. The family supports both display and body text use, with three slant options and variable font technology for precise control of weight and slant.

name: Hagrid
classification_notes: A grotesque sans that intentionally blends inverted contrast, calligraphic counterspaces and brutalist forms into its structure, making it a quirky but grounded member of the grotesque tradition.
summary: A grotesque sans by Cosimo Lorenzo Pancini that draws on vernacular and experimental letterform traditions to build a family with genuine typographic personality across display and text cuts.
description: Built on grotesque structure but deliberately inflected with calligraphic counterspaces, inverted contrast and brutalist shapes, it balances oddness and usability across 27 static styles and four variable fonts. Originally a display-only release from 2019, a 2025 expansion added the Hagrid Text subfamily with refined proportions and optimised spacing, plus a new Black style with fully closed counterspaces. Script support spans Extended Latin, Cyrillic, Greek and Arabic, and a Monsters stylistic set transforms individual letters into creature-like forms.
foundry_blurb: Zetafonts describe Hagrid as a celebration of the expressive potential of grotesque letterforms filtered through a crypto-typographer's love of the strange and overlooked. The family blends grotesque structure with calligraphic counterspaces, inverted contrast and brutalist shapes, and was expanded in 2025 to include a Text subfamily with improved readability and an extended Black style, while maintaining support for Extended Latin, Cyrillic, Greek and Arabic.

name: Tarif
classification_notes: Tarif is explicitly described as a slab serif with a humanist skeleton and inverted contrast, mixing calligraphic details with postmodern grotesque letter shapes.
summary: A humanist slab serif with inverted contrast and calligraphic details, designed by Andrea Tartarelli and inspired by the multicultural coexistence of tenth-century Andalusia.
description: Built on a humanist skeleton, Tarif combines extreme inktraps, calligraphic details and an unorthodox slab construction with inverted contrast to produce a design that sits comfortably between display and editorial use. Seven weights from Extralight to Extrabold, each with a matching italic, cover Latin and Cyrillic across 217 languages, with a separate Arabic version developed with Rania Azmi. Variable font axes for both roman and italic weights are included with the full family package.
foundry_blurb: Zetafonts describe Tarif as a slab serif typeface inspired by Convivencia, the peaceful coexistence of Muslims, Christians and Jews in tenth-century Andalusia, which they credit with bringing Greek philosophy and Muslim culture to Europe. Designed by Andrea Tartarelli with italics by Cosimo Lorenzo Pancini, the family mixes Latin calligraphic details, extreme inktraps and a postmodern reinvention of grotesque letterforms, and is complemented by an Arabic version in which the inverted contrast finds a natural connection with modern Arabic letterforms.

name: Duepuntozero
classification_notes: A monolinear geometric sans with strictly modular construction and rounded corners; its condensed proportions and uniform stroke weight place it firmly in the geometric sans category.
summary: A monolinear geometric sans with rounded corners and condensed proportions, originally conceived as a logo typeface in 2004 and fully expanded in 2019 to cover Latin, Greek and Cyrillic across nine weights with matching italics and variable fonts.
description: Originally created as a companion to the rounded display family Arista, Duepuntozero was rebuilt in 2019 as a fully featured pro family with nine weights from Thin to Heavy, each with italics, plus two variable font files. The strictly modular, monolinear construction and compact proportions suit mobile interface and app design, where the condensed forms save screen space without sacrificing legibility. A matching icon set across the same nine weights is included to support consistent branding across infographics and digital products.
foundry_blurb: Zetafonts describe Duepuntozero as a classic from their catalogue, originally a logo typeface from 2004 that was entirely redesigned in 2019, gaining Cyrillic and Greek support, four extra weights, italics, variable fonts and a full icon subfamily to assist designers building consistent visual systems.

name: Cocomat Pro
designer: Francesco Canovaro, Debora Manetti, Cosimo Lorenzo Pancini
classification_notes: A geometric sans with humanistic proportions, low contrast and slightly rounded corners. Art deco-influenced uppercase letterforms with vertical unbalancing place it firmly in the geometric sans category with decorative leanings.
summary: A geometric sans-serif with art deco-inflected uppercase letterforms and humanistic proportions, part of Zetafonts' wider Coco Gothic system.
description: Designed by Francesco Canovaro and Debora Manetti, Cocomat draws on the geometric skeleton shared across the Coco Gothic family but introduces vertical unbalancing in uppercase letters such as E, A, F, P and R that recalls 1920s and 1930s lettering. Lowercase stems carry optical compensation, softening the overall rhythm. The Pro revision of 2019 expanded the family to 18 styles across nine weights with matching italics, added OpenType features including small caps, oldstyle figures and stylistic alternates, and extended coverage to over 218 languages across Latin, Greek and Cyrillic scripts.
foundry_blurb: Zetafonts describe Cocomat as a development of the Coco Gothic typeface system, distinguished from its siblings by art deco-inspired uppercase detailing and optical stem compensation in the lowercase. Originally released in 2014, the typeface was fully redesigned in 2019 as Cocomat Pro, gaining eight additional weights, expanded OpenType features and multilingual support across more than 200 languages.

name: Monterchi
designer: Cosimo Lorenzo Pancini, Riccardo Falcinelli, Andrea Tartarelli, Maria Chiara Fantini
classification_notes: A classical Roman display serif rooted in early Renaissance letterforms, reworked from Francesco Canovaro's Beatrix Antiqua with geometric construction and humanist proportions. The family extends to a text cut, a slab serif variant and a sans companion sharing the same geometric skeleton.
summary: A 50-style type system commissioned for the rebranding of Piero della Francesca's Monterchi fresco, built on classical Roman geometry with an extensive ligature palette.
description: Originating as a custom commission for the 2019 rebranding of a Renaissance fresco site, the family reworks Francesco Canovaro's Beatrix Antiqua into four subfamilies: a display serif, a text-optimised cut with larger x-height and wider spacing, a slab serif variant, and a geometric sans. All share a common classical skeleton with over 100 discretionary ligatures, small caps, and stylistic alternates across more than 1,100 glyphs covering 217 languages in Latin, Greek and Cyrillic. The range suits editorial, signage, logo and wayfinding work where a historically grounded but flexible system is needed.
foundry_blurb: Zetafonts describe Monterchi as a 50-weight ode to classical Roman letterforms, designed as a rework of Beatrix Antiqua for the 2019 rebranding of the Monterchi fresco site by Art Director Riccardo Falcinelli. The system pairs elegant alternates and discretionary ligatures with a companion text family, a serif variant and a sans, all sharing the same geometric structure, with an extended character set covering over 200 languages in Latin, Greek and Cyrillic.

name: Kitsch
designer: Francesco Canovaro, Andrea Tartarelli, Maria Chiara Fantini
classification_notes: Kitsch merges classical roman proportions with broad-nib calligraphic strokes derived from medieval gothic letterforms, producing high contrast and spiky details that place it firmly in the display category with a calligraphic subcategory.
summary: A high-contrast calligraphic typeface crossing classical roman proportions with broad-nib gothic letterforms, available in display and text optical cuts across eight weights.
description: Designed by Francesco Canovaro with Andrea Tartarelli and Maria Chiara Fantini, Kitsch draws on the broad-nib pen to bridge classical roman skeletons and medieval gothic scripts without directly quoting historical models such as Rotunda or Bastarda. The result is a spiky, high-contrast face that works at poster scale in its display cut, while the separately tuned text version preserves that expressivity down to smaller sizes. All 32 styles cover Latin, Greek and Cyrillic across 218 languages, and OpenType features include small caps, positional numerals, stylistic alternates and discretionary ligatures.
foundry_blurb: Zetafonts describe Kitsch as a typeface living at the crossroads of classical latin and medieval gothic letterforms, finding a contemporary vibe through the dynamic texture of the calligraphic broad-nib pen applied to classical roman proportions. Born for editorial and branding use, they call it fashionable but solid, self-confident enough to look classic while ironic enough to be contemporary.

name: Aquawax Pro
classification_notes: Aquawax Pro is a geometric sans-serif with open, modernist letterforms and a generous x-height; its rounded edges and distinctive logo-derived details such as the blade-tailed Q and reversed W sit firmly within the geometric sans tradition.
summary: Originally created as a custom brand typeface in 2008, this geometric sans-serif carries distinctive logo-design details — a blade-tailed Q, fin-legged K and reversed W — across nine weights with matching italics.
description: Designed by Francesco Canovaro and first released commercially in 2015, the family gained wide recognition after Warner Bros used it for the Aquaman logo. A 2019 redesign by the Zetafonts team expanded the original six weights to nine, added full italic variants, introduced Cyrillic and Greek coverage across 218 languages, and incorporated OpenType features including small caps, old-style figures and multiple stylistic sets. The open letterforms and generous x-height keep it readable at text sizes despite its origins in display and identity work.
foundry_blurb: Zetafonts describe Aquawax as one of their most successful typefaces, originally created as a custom brand font in 2008 and later chosen by Warner Bros for the Aquaman movie logo. The Pro version, released in 2019, was a full redesign adding italics, Cyrillic and Greek scripts, pictogram styles, a variable font, and an expanded OpenType feature set including small caps, positional figures, discretionary ligatures and stylistic alternates, covering over 200 languages.

name: Iconic
designer: Andrea Tartarelli, Cosimo Lorenzo Pancini, Francesco Canovaro
classification_notes: Rounded, condensed sans-serif with letterforms derived from basic circular geometry; humanist touches keep it readable across text and display sizes.
summary: A rounded condensed geometric sans with stencil and pictogram subfamilies, built for editorial, interface and wayfinding across 219 languages.
description: Designed by Andrea Tartarelli, Cosimo Lorenzo Pancini and Francesco Canovaro, Iconic draws its letterforms from circular geometry while small humanist details soften its technical rigidity. Thirty styles span eight weights from Thin to Heavy with matching italics, a six-weight Stencil subfamily offering an aggressive counterpart, and a matched six-weight Pictograms set for interface work. Coverage extends to over 1,200 glyphs across Latin, Greek and Cyrillic scripts, with OpenType features including small caps, oldstyle figures and multiple stylistic sets.
foundry_blurb: Zetafonts describe Iconic as a multi-purpose workhorse family for editorial, interface and information design, where space efficiency is key. The soft circular geometry gains informal humanist touches for better readability, while the Stencil subfamily adds a more technical aesthetic suitable for wayfinding and branding. A six-weight Pictograms set allows designers to maintain visual consistency across full digital interface projects.

name: Radcliffe Hand
designer: Cosimo Lorenzo Pancini, Giulia Ursenna Dorati
classification_notes: Each glyph is a hand-traced interpretation of the Radcliffe serif family, giving it calligraphic, handwriting qualities. The connected cursive Script variant confirms classification as a script/handwritten typeface rather than a conventional serif.
summary: A hand-traced revival of Zetafonts' Radcliffe family, reinterpreting its humanist skeleton as calligraphic letterforms across four weights plus a connected Script variant.
description: Designed by Cosimo Lorenzo Pancini with Giulia Ursenna Dorati, the family sits between type and handwriting, with each glyph individually traced from the original Radcliffe design to preserve legibility while gaining expressive, calligraphic character. The four upright and italic weights suit both text and display use, while the connected cursive Radcliffe Hand Script can be paired with the broader Radcliffe family for layered handwriting effects. Over 400 glyphs cover 213 languages, with OpenType features including discretionary ligatures for a set of hand-drawn arrows.
foundry_blurb: Zetafonts describe Radcliffe Hand as a reinvention of their Radcliffe family as a handwritten typeface, with every glyph lovingly hand-traced to translate design qualities into calligraphic quirks. They highlight its humanist skeleton and dynamic handwriting qualities as enabling use at both display and text sizes, and note its suitability for editorial and packaging contexts. The family includes a connected cursive Script variant that pairs with both Radcliffe Hand and the standard Radcliffe and Radcliffe Casual.

name: Cocogoose Classic
designer: Francesco Canovaro, Cosimo Lorenzo Pancini, Andrea Tartarelli
classification_notes: Described explicitly as a geometric sans serif with very low contrast and slightly rounded corners; the Classic subfamily introduces a smaller x-height and round dots while retaining the geometric uppercase structure of the original Cocogoose.
summary: A geometric sans with slightly rounded corners and a reduced x-height, developed in 2019 as a refined subfamily of the original Cocogoose with expanded OpenType features and over 1000 glyphs.
description: Cocogoose Classic branches off the original 2012 Cocogoose design, retaining the geometric uppercase forms while introducing a new lowercase with a smaller x-height and round dots. Eight weights from Thin to Black each come with a matching italic, and the glyph set exceeds 1000 characters with coverage across Latin, Greek and Cyrillic scripts for 218 languages. OpenType features include small capitals, positional numerals, discretionary ligatures and stylistic alternates.
foundry_blurb: Zetafonts describe Cocogoose Classic as a 2019 subfamily created by Francesco Canovaro, Andrea Tartarelli and Cosimo Lorenzo Pancini, building on the original Cocogoose design first produced in 2012. It keeps the uppercase design intact while developing a new lowercase with a smaller x-height and round dots, and expands the glyph count to over 1000 characters with Greek and Cyrillic support alongside extended OpenType features including positional numerals, alternate forms and ligatures.

name: Blackest
classification_notes: Blackest is an inverse contrast (Italian-style) wedge serif, where horizontal strokes are heavier than verticals, inverting the typical modulation of classical serifs. It derives from Blacker, a wedge serif, but shifts the contrast axis entirely.
summary: A reverse-contrast wedge serif derived from Cosimo Lorenzo Pancini's Blacker, inverting the stroke modulation to produce a quirky yet classical display family.
description: Built on the skeleton of Blacker, this family flips conventional contrast so horizontal strokes outweigh verticals, placing it in the tradition of so-called Italian or reverse-contrast typefaces. Six display weights from Light to Heavy suit editorial, poster and identity work, while a two-weight Text variant offers reduced contrast and looser spacing for longer-form setting. The character set covers over 220 Latin-based languages and Russian Cyrillic, with OpenType features including small caps, stylistic alternates, old-style figures and discretionary ligatures.
foundry_blurb: Zetafonts describe Blackest as a development of the Blacker typeface, retaining its classical skeleton and sharp wedge serifs while transposing the contrast into the reverse-contrast or 'Italian' register. The result, they say, is quirky but classical, playful without losing elegance, and suited to display use across editorial, poster and logo work, with a text variant added for longer typesetting.

name: Holden
classification_notes: Holden draws on pointed brush aesthetics with high stroke contrast and curvy, calligraphic forms across an extreme weight range, placing it firmly in the contrasted serif tradition with a contemporary editorial sensibility.
summary: A six-weight Latin serif with pointed brush roots and high stroke contrast, designed to span text and display use within a single family.
description: Designed by Cosimo Lorenzo Pancini and Andrea Tartarelli, Holden runs from a lightly contrasted Light through to a fluid, tight-countered Fat, with matching italics across all six weights. The curvy, brush-influenced letterforms hold up in running text at the lighter end and deliver expressive results at display sizes as the weight increases. Each font includes a thorough set of OpenType features: small caps, stylistic alternates, swash italics, old-style figures and a set of decorative manicules and fleurons.
foundry_blurb: Zetafonts describe Holden as a research into texture and extreme weight range, with curvy shapes inspired by pointed brush aesthetics. The family is aimed at editorial and packaging work where a contrasting range of weights is needed to avoid monotony while keeping branding consistent, covering over 214 languages and including decorative dingbats such as manicules and fleurons.

name: Groot
classification_notes: Groot is firmly in the industrial grotesque tradition, drawing on DIN-like proportions while introducing a variable curvature axis and brutalist uppercase alternates that push it towards constructed, mechanical expressiveness.
summary: A variable industrial grotesque by Cosimo Lorenzo Pancini that uses a curvature axis to shift between utilitarian body text and expressive display settings.
description: Designed at Zetafonts by Cosimo Lorenzo Pancini with Mario De Libero and Nicolas Sendre Billout, Groot takes DIN-like proportions as its base and introduces a variable axis controlling the superness of inner-counter curvature. The Bau subfamily pushes this to its limits, producing overlapping counters in thin weights and squared counterspaces in heavy ones, creating a noticeably contrasted result. Brutalist uppercase alternates and six stylistic sets give the family range from transparent body text to logotype and editorial work, with 20 static styles and two variable font files across the full package.
foundry_blurb: Zetafonts describe Groot as a reinvention of the traditional industrial grotesque that adds a variable axis for dynamic control of curvature in the inner space. The Bau subfamily pushes that variation to extremes, while the full superfamily can shift between a transparent utilitarian sans for body text and a quirky display font suited to logos and editorial use. The typeface won a Communication Arts award for Typeface Design in 2024.

name: Cocosignum
classification_notes: Cocosignum blends imperial Art Deco uppercase lettering with a geometric cursive lowercase, placing it firmly in the display category with a distinct Italian 1930s propaganda-deco aesthetic.
summary: An Art Deco display family drawing on Italian 1930s typography, pairing imperial propagandist uppercase forms with a geometric cursive lowercase.
description: Designed by Cosimo Lorenzo Pancini and released in 2017 as part of the Christmas Vibes set, Cocosignum comes in two distinct subfamilies: Corsivo Italico, which combines the monumental uppercase with a cursive geometric script lowercase, and Maiuscoletto, which leans into the small-capitals register. Both run across five weights from Ultralight to Heavy, and the family covers 205 languages across Latin, Greek and Cyrillic scripts. The propaganda-deco overtones of the uppercase give it a period flavour suited to packaging, branding and editorial work with a retro Italian sensibility.
foundry_blurb: Zetafonts describe Cocosignum as taking inspiration from the typography of the Italian 1930s, with imperial uppercase letterforms carrying propaganda-deco overtones softened by a cursive geometric lowercase in the Corsivo Italico version. The family ships in two styles and five weights, covering over 205 languages across Latin, Greek and Cyrillic scripts.

name: Sugo
classification_notes: A condensed geometric sans-serif with slightly rounded corners, originating as a logo typeface and expanded into a full family. The geometric construction and tight proportions place it firmly in the geometric sans category.
summary: A condensed geometric sans originally designed as a logo typeface in 2006, fully redesigned in 2018 with expanded language support, italics and a companion Display subfamily.
description: Originally drawn by Francesco Canovaro in 2006, Sugo is a condensed geometric sans with slightly rounded corners built for logo and branding work. Andrea Tartarelli undertook a complete redesign in 2018, adding three extra weights, italics, Cyrillic and Greek, small caps and full OpenType features. The family splits into Sugo Pro Classic for general and body use and Sugo Pro Display, which uses alternate glyphs and tighter kerning suited to titling and identity work.
foundry_blurb: Zetafonts describe Sugo as one of their oldest and most loved typefaces, created as a logo typeface by Francesco Canovaro in 2006 with a no-nonsense street style attitude. The 2018 redesign by Andrea Tartarelli expanded the family to 20 styles across Classic and Display subfamilies, adding Cyrillic and Greek coverage, italics, small caps and OpenType features including stylistic alternates drawn from Canovaro's original designs.

name: Body
classification_notes: Body is a modernist grotesque superfamily with two distinct subfamilies: Body Grotesque, which introduces calligraphic quirks and inverse contrast drawn from early 20th-century specimens, and Body Text, which prioritises readability through open terminals and maximised horizontal strokes.
summary: A 64-style grotesque superfamily in two variants — Text and Grotesque — each offered across four widths and four weights, designed as a contemporary alternative to Univers or Helvetica.
description: Body Text prioritises legibility through open terminals and strong horizontal strokes, countering the darkness typical of modernist grotesques. Body Grotesque takes a different path, introducing subtly calligraphic curves and a light inverse contrast at bold weights that gives display sizes a faint vintage character. Both subfamilies cover 204 languages via Latin Extended and Russian Cyrillic, with small caps, oldstyle figures and multiple OpenType features throughout.
foundry_blurb: Zetafonts describe Body as a contemporary alternative to modernist superfamilies like Univers or Helvetica, designed by Cosimo Lorenzo Pancini with Andrea Tartarelli to maximise text readability across a wide range of typographic situations. The family splits into Body Text and Body Grotesque, each in four widths — Slim, standard, Fit and Large — and four weights from Light to Extrabold, totalling 64 fonts with full Latin Extended and Cyrillic coverage, small caps, positional numbers and stylistic alternates.

name: Radcliffe
designer: Cosimo Lorenzo Pancini, Francesco Canovaro, Andrea Tartarelli
classification_notes: Radcliffe is explicitly described as a reinvention of traditional Clarendon design, featuring strong bracketed serifs, vertical stress and low contrast characteristic of the Clarendon subcategory.
summary: A 26-style Clarendon revival with a humanist inflection, covering display, text and a quirky reverse-contrast Casual subfamily.
description: Designed by Cosimo Lorenzo Pancini and Andrea Tartarelli in 2018, Radcliffe reworks the Clarendon model with bracketed serifs, vertical stress and low contrast, softened by calligraphic details most visible in the italics. Radcliffe Text offers a taller x-height and slightly condensed proportions for body copy, while the two-weight Radcliffe Casual introduces a reverse-contrast aesthetic for more playful applications. All cuts include comprehensive OpenType features and coverage for over 200 languages across Latin and Cyrillic scripts.
foundry_blurb: Zetafonts describe Radcliffe as a reinvention of traditional Clarendon design in pursuit of a contemporary classic look, tailor-made for elegance, and suited to editorial and logo-design work. The Text subfamily was developed specifically for long-form reading on screen, and the Casual weights bring a reverse-contrast aesthetic to the family.

name: DoubleBass
classification_notes: Explicitly created as a display typeface drawing on 1950s cartoon-modern lettering and Saul Bass's cut-out hand lettering style; its modular, alternating-cap structure is purely display-oriented.
summary: A four-weight display family by Cosimo Lorenzo Pancini drawing on Saul Bass's cut-out lettering and 1950s cartoon-modern graphics, using OpenType alternating capitals to produce a natural bouncing rhythm without extensive ligature sets.
description: Designed in homage to Saul Bass's title sequence work and the jazzy graphics of the mid-twentieth century, the family uses a modular structure in which two alternating sets of capitals and numbers are substituted automatically via OpenType, producing a lively up-down rhythm as you type. Four weights run from Thin through to Bold, with lighter cuts serviceable for short text blocks and heavier weights suited to logotypes and headlines. Latin and Cyrillic writing systems are covered across 198 languages and 432 glyphs per style.
foundry_blurb: Zetafonts describe DoubleBass as a jazzy four-weight display family inspired by Saul Bass's iconic hand lettering for The Man with the Golden Arm and the cartoon-modern lettering of the 1950s. Rather than relying on a large ligature set, the alternating capital sets and numbers create a bouncy rhythm automatically through OpenType substitution, giving designers an out-of-the-box tool for retro logo and heading work.

name: Freehand Brush
classification_notes: A freestyle brush cursive that uses OpenType ligature substitution to randomise character alternates, mimicking the irregularity of genuine handmade brush lettering. The system also includes a complementary blockletter subfamily.
summary: A brush script type system that uses OpenType ligature substitution to randomise character variants, producing a convincingly uneven, handmade appearance.
description: Designed by Debora Manetti and Francesco Canovaro, the system centres on a wide freestyle brush cursive with over 400 character alternates and double-letter ligatures to avoid the repetitive look typical of digital script fonts. A companion Brush Easy version disables the substitutions for consistent output, while two Blockletter weights provide contrasting upright styles for mixed typesetting. Two icon sets, one for food and one for UI navigation, round out the six-style family. Coverage extends to 201 Latin-script languages.
foundry_blurb: Zetafonts describe Freehand as a type system built to emulate natural handmade brush writing, using OpenType ligature substitutions to randomly cycle through character variants for a realistic, uneven result. The family pairs the main brush cursive and its easier variant with two blockletter weights for contrast, plus matching food and UI icon sets, and covers over 40 Latin-alphabet languages with full accents and diacritics.

name: Heading Pro
designer: Francesco Canovaro, Andrea Tartarelli, Cosimo Lorenzo Pancini, Chiara Ghezzi
classification_notes: A geometric-leaning sans-serif with post-modernist attention to detail, designed primarily for display and titling use across an unusually wide range of widths from ultra-compressed to ultra-wide.
summary: A large sans-serif system spanning eight widths and nine weights, built for space-optimised titling and expressive headline work.
description: Originally designed by Francesco Canovaro and expanded by the Zetafonts team into 146 variants, the family runs from Ultra Compressed to Ultra Wide with nine weights at each width. The condensed cuts suit credit lists and timetables where space is tight, while the wide cuts trade economy for raw presence in headlines. Each style covers Latin, Cyrillic and Greek across 218 languages, and includes small caps, old-style figures and stylistic alternates; variable font versions are available with weight and width axes.
foundry_blurb: Zetafonts describe Heading Pro as a versatile sans-serif type system built around eight widths from ultra-compressed to ultra-wide and nine weights from thin to black, totalling 146 variants. The condensed styles are suited to space-saving titles, timetables and credit lists, while the wide cuts are intended for bold, expressive headlines where style takes priority over economy. Each weight includes over 800 characters with coverage for 100+ languages in Latin, Cyrillic and Greek, plus a full OpenType feature set including small caps, stylistic alternates and multiple numeral styles.

name: Studio Gothic
designer: Andrea Tartarelli, Francesco Canovaro, Cosimo Lorenzo Pancini
classification_notes: Rooted in the geometric modernist tradition via Futura, but filtered through Alessandro Butti's Semplicità, giving it a slightly warmer, more individual character than a pure geometric sans.
summary: A geometric sans in eight weights drawing on Alessandro Butti's 1930s Semplicità rather than Futura directly, with an alternate subfamily offering additional letterform variations.
description: Designed by Andrea Tartarelli with Cosimo Lorenzo Pancini and Francesco Canovaro, the family revisits the spare proportions of Butti's Semplicità while introducing its own takes on letter shapes and spacing. Italics carry slight calligraphic traces that make them suitable for display and logotype work in their own right. The Alternate subfamily adds further glyph variations across all eight weights, and the full set covers over 200 languages across Latin, Greek and Cyrillic scripts.
foundry_blurb: Zetafonts describe Studio Gothic as more a reinvention than a revival of Butti's Semplicità, aiming for a typeface that reads as classic yet unexpected, slightly modernist but still contemporary. The companion italics feature calligraphic touches suited to display and logo use, while the Alternate family extends the range of letterform choices further.

name: Bimbo Classic
classification_notes: A monoline script family designed to mimic handwritten marks from different pen tools; its even stroke weight and casual letterforms place it firmly in the monoline handwritten script category.
summary: A monoline handwritten script family in seven styles, each named after a different pen or marker instrument, covering over 200 languages across Latin, Greek and Cyrillic scripts.
description: Designed by Francesco Canovaro in 2018, Bimbo Classic grew from an earlier collaboration with illustrator Jonathan Calugi on the Arsenale White typeface, expanding that design with six new weights and over 300 additional characters. The seven styles — from Ballpoint through to Dripping Jumbo — simulate the stroke quality of different writing tools, while open counters and a consistent monoline construction keep the letterforms legible at small sizes. A built-in OpenType letter-swapping feature ensures repeated characters never appear identical, preserving the irregularity of genuine handwriting.
foundry_blurb: Zetafonts describe Bimbo Classic as a monoline script family by Francesco Canovaro, conceived as an extension and redesign of the original Arsenale White typeface made with illustrator Jonathan Calugi. The name references the Italian word for 'baby', and the family was built to combine a handmade aesthetic with broad multilingual reach, covering over 200 languages and adding open-type letter-swapping to keep doubled characters visually distinct.

name: Anaphora
designer: Francesco Canovaro, Andrea Tartarelli, Cosimo Lorenzo Pancini
classification_notes: Anaphora uses triangular wedge serifs rather than bracketed or hairline forms, placing it in the Latin wedge or lapidary serif tradition. Its wide counters and low x-height give it a contemporary editorial character distinct from classical old-style or transitional models.
summary: A wedge serif with nine weights, true italics and a stencil sub-family, covering Latin, Greek and Cyrillic across 200 languages.
description: Designed by Francesco Canovaro with Cosimo Lorenzo Pancini and Andrea Tartarelli, Anaphora pairs wide counters and a low x-height for comfortable reading at text sizes with distinctive letterforms that hold presence at display sizes. The 18-style serif set spans Thin to Fat with matching true italics, while four stencil cuts in Thin, Book, ExtraBold and Fat extend its use into titling and logo work. OpenType features include small caps, four figure sets, fractions, stylistic alternates and discretionary ligatures.
foundry_blurb: Zetafonts describe Anaphora as a contemporary serif with wedge serifs and nine weights from thin to fat, each accompanied by a true italic, suited to editorial and advertising work. Wide counters and a low x-height make it readable at text sizes, while distinctive shapes give it presence at display sizes. Four stencil weights add options for titling and logo use, and the extended character set covers over 200 languages across Latin, Greek and Cyrillic scripts.

name: Cocotte
classification_notes: Described explicitly as a small caps sans serif display typeface, Cocotte draws on Art Nouveau and Jugendstil geometric sources, placing it firmly in the display category with a geometric sans construction.
summary: A small caps display sans in three weights drawing on Arts and Crafts, Jugendstil and French Art Nouveau sources, offered in two stylistic variants.
description: Cocotte comes in a Regular style rooted in geometric Jugendstil and an Alternate style closer to French and Italian Art Nouveau, each with matching italics across Extralight, Regular and Heavy weights. It sits within Zetafonts' broader Coco Gothic family as the 1900 and 1910 historical variants, part of a decade-by-decade encyclopaedia of geometric sans styles. The character set covers 205 languages across Latin, Greek and Cyrillic scripts.
foundry_blurb: Zetafonts describe Cocotte as a small caps sans serif display typeface inspired by early Art Nouveau graphic style, released in 2017 and designed by Cosimo Lorenzo Pancini. It forms part of the Coco Gothic family, representing the 1900 and 1910 historical versions of that typeface, with an extended character set supporting over forty European languages plus Greek and Cyrillic.

name: Altair
designer: Francesco Canovaro, Cosimo Lorenzo Pancini, Andrea Tartarelli
